---
title: "Mapping the processes leading to ecological novelty"
author: "Alejandro Ordonez"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    number-sections: true
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
editor: visual
bibliography: references.bib
csl: science.csl
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

This document presents the scripts used to construct [***Figure 2***]{.underline}, as referenced in **Ordonez et al. (in preparation)**. The mapping is grounded in the idea that understanding core community assembly processes—dispersal, abiotic filtering, biotic interactions, and ecological drift—offers a systematic framework for anticipating the emergence of novel ecosystems and their consequences. By thoroughly analysing the components of the community assembly machinery and how they interrelate, we can improve our predictions of the ecological novelty that arises from human influence. This understanding will also enable us to more effectively navigate the processes that guide us toward a desired future environmental state.

## Mapping Dispersal Limitation {#sec-dispersal}

Mapping the role of dispersal in the formation of novel ecosystems involves assessing the capacity of species, functional groups, or communities to track changing environmental conditions (i.e. does dispersal capacity match the rate of ecological change?). It also involves evaluating changes in colonisation dynamics and alterations in connectivity across landscapes.

To illustrate this, we have chosen map the overall mismatch between shifts in climatic conditions (i.e., Climate displacement cf. [@ordonez2016]) and dispersal distances derived from dispersal kernels or probabilities.

Climate displacement refers to the velocity at which combined climate isoclines—representing lines of equal climatic conditions—shift across geographic areas due to alterations in temperature and precipitation over time. Displacement is quantified using the speed of climate velocity vectors (@fig-VelEstimate).

```{r}
#| label: Velestimation
#| eval: false

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 0: PACKAGE LOADING
# ==============================================================================

# --- 1a. Package loading ------------------------------------------------------

library(terra)              # Raster and vector spatial data handling
library(tidyterra)          # terra–ggplot2 integration
terraOptions(progress = 0)  # Suppress progress bars in rendered output
library(tidyverse)          # Data manipulation and visualisation
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth country polygons as sf objects
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Required data for rnaturalearth

# Load custom velocity functions (SpatHetFnc, VelocityFnc)
# SpatHetFnc: estimates the spatial gradient (∇X) of a climate variable
# VelocityFnc: divides temporal gradient (ΔX/yr) by spatial gradient (∇X)
source("./Displacement/Functions/VelocityFnc.R")


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: LOAD AND PREPARE CLIMATE DATA
# ==============================================================================

# --- 1a. Baseline climate (historical: ~1970–2000)-----------------------------

# --- 1b. Load seasonal means for temperature and precipitation from CCSM4------
# Layers 1–4: Seasonal mean temperature (°C) for MAM / JJA / SON / DJF
# Layers 5–8: Seasonal total precipitation (mm) for MAM / JJA / SON / DJF
clim_vars_now <- c(
  rast("./Displacement/Historical/CCSM4/CCSM4_1970_to_2000_Tm.Seasonal.tif"),
  rast("./Displacement/Historical/CCSM4/CCSM4_1970_to_2000_P.Seasonal.tif")
)
names(clim_vars_now) <- c(
  paste0("Temp_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF")),
  paste0("Prec_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF"))
)

# --- 1c. Future climate (2085–2115, RCP 6.0, CCSM4)----------------------------

clim_vars_fut <- c(
  rast("./Displacement/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_2085_to_2115_Tm.Seasonal.tif"),
  rast("./Displacement/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_2085_to_2115_P.Seasonal.tif")
)
names(clim_vars_fut) <- c(
  paste0("Temp_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF")),
  paste0("Prec_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF"))
)


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: REPROJECT AND RESAMPLE TO EQUAL-AREA GRID
# ==============================================================================

# The Eckert IV projection (+proj=eck4) is area-preserving, which is essential
# for computing spatial gradients in physically meaningful units (km).

# --- 2a. Project baseline and future rasters to Eckert IV----------------------
StartPeriodPrj <- project(clim_vars_now, "+proj=eck4", method = "near", threads = 50)
EndPeriodPrj   <- project(clim_vars_fut, "+proj=eck4", method = "near", threads = 50)

# --- 2b. Create a 50 × 50 km template raster in Eckert IV----------------------
# 50 km is a reasonable grain for global-scale displacement analysis and
# avoids artificial precision given GCM output resolutions (~100–200 km)
eck4Rast <- rast(
  extent     = ext(StartPeriodPrj),
  resolution = 50000,   # 50,000 m = 50 km
  crs        = "+proj=eck4"
)

# --- 2c. Resample both periods to the 50 × 50 km grid--------------------------
# Temperature layers (1–4) rounded to 1 decimal; precipitation (5–8) to 0 (integer mm)

StartPeriod_50x50 <- lapply(1:dim(StartPeriodPrj)[3], function(x) {
  round(resample(StartPeriodPrj[[x]], eck4Rast, threads = 50),
        ifelse(x >= 5, 0, 1))
})

EndPeriod_50x50 <- lapply(1:dim(EndPeriodPrj)[3], function(x) {
  round(resample(EndPeriodPrj[[x]], eck4Rast, threads = 50),
        ifelse(x >= 5, 0, 1))
})


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 3: COMPUTE CLIMATE VELOCITY COMPONENTS
# ==============================================================================
# For each climate variable × season combination, climate velocity is:
#   V = (ΔX / Δt) / ∇X
# where:
#   ΔX / Δt  = temporal gradient = change per year (future − baseline) / 50 yrs
#   ∇X       = spatial gradient  = local slope of the climate surface (units/km)

# --- 3a. Spatial gradient (∇X) for baseline climate----------------------------
# SpatHetFnc computes the magnitude of the local spatial gradient using a
# focal window, with the cell resolution supplied in km (here 50 km)
SpatHetTmp <- lapply(StartPeriod_50x50, function(x) {
  SpatHetFnc(RastIn = x, Dist = res(x)[1] / 1000)  # convert m → km
})

# --- 3b. Temporal gradient (ΔX / Δt)-------------------------------------------
# Divided by 50 to express as change per year (2085–2115 midpoint minus
# 1970–2000 midpoint spans approximately 100 years; here using the 50-yr
# half-interval consistent with centred midpoints at 1985 and 2100)
TimeHetAnomaly <- lapply(1:length(EndPeriod_50x50), function(x) {
  (EndPeriod_50x50[[x]] - StartPeriod_50x50[[x]]) / 50
})

# --- 3c. Scalar velocity for each variable × season----------------------------
# VelocityFnc divides temporal by spatial gradient (Loarie et al. 2009)
VelocityAnomaly <- lapply(1:length(EndPeriod_50x50), function(x) {
  VelocityFnc(TimeHetAnomaly[[x]], SpatHetTmp[[x]])
})

# --- 3d. Combine into a single multi-layer raster and restore variable names---
clim_vel_2100 <- do.call("c", VelocityAnomaly)
names(clim_vel_2100) <- names(clim_vars_now)

# --- 3e. Save the raster-------------------------------------------------------
writeRaster(clim_vel_2100,
            "./Displacement/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_1970_to_2000_Velocity.Seasonal.tif",
            overwrite =TRUE)

clim_vel_2100a

# --- 3f. Plot------------------------------------------------------------------
# --- 3g. Load and project global vector data-----------------------------------
world <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")) 
# Retrieves country polygons at medium resolution and converts them to a 'SpatVector'

wrld_simpl2 <- project(world,"+proj=eck4")
# Projects the world map into Eckert IV projection, suitable for global thematic maps

ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +
  
  # Raster layer: log10-transformed displacement for display
  # log10 transformation linearises the colour scale across orders of magnitude
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(clim_vel_2100)) +
  facet_wrap(~lyr) +
  
  # Colour scale with interpretable legend labels in original units (km/yr)
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",       # Muted, perceptually uniform palette
    na.value = NA,           # Transparent where data are missing (ocean, ice, etc.)
    breaks = -2:2,           # Log10 breakpoints: 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 km/yr
    labels = c("<0.01", "0.1", "1", "10", ">100"),
    name = "Displacement [km/yr]",
    limit = c(-2, 2),        # Clip colour scale to 0.01–100 km/yr range
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = 15,
      barheight      = 0.5
    )
  ) +
  
  # Overlay country borders (transparent fill, outline only)
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +
  
  labs(
    title    = "Climate Velocity for 2100 under RCP 6.0",
    subtitle = "Seasonal climate velocity under RCP 6.0 (CCSM4) · Eckert IV equal-area projection"
  ) +
  
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-VelEstimate
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Estimate of the speed (in km per year) of seasonal climate variables between 2000 and 2100 under RCP 6.0 for CCSM4.
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 8

library(terra)              # Raster and vector spatial data handling
library(tidyterra)          # terra–ggplot2 integration
terraOptions(progress = 0)  # Suppress progress bars in rendered output
library(tidyverse)          # Data manipulation and visualisation
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth country polygons as sf objects
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Required data for rnaturalearth

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: COMPUTE CLIMATE VELOCITY COMPONENTS
# ==============================================================================

# --- 1a. # Load the Scalar velocity for each variable × season-----------------

clim_vel_2100 <- rast("./Displacement/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_1970_to_2000_Velocity.Seasonal.tif")
clim_vel_2100 <- app(clim_vel_2100,function(x){ifelse(x<0.01,0.01,
                                                      ifelse(x>100,100,x))})

# --- 1b. Load and project global vector data-----------------------------------
world <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")) 
# Retrieves country polygons at medium resolution and converts them to a 'SpatVector'

wrld_simpl2 <- project(world,"+proj=eck4")
# Projects the world map into Eckert IV projection, suitable for global thematic maps

# --- 1c. Plot------------------------------------------------------------------

ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +
  
  # Raster layer: log10-transformed displacement for display
  # log10 transformation linearises the colour scale across orders of magnitude
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(clim_vel_2100)) +
  facet_wrap(~lyr) +
  
  # Colour scale with interpretable legend labels in original units (km/yr)
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",       # Muted, perceptually uniform palette
    na.value = NA,           # Transparent where data are missing (ocean, ice, etc.)
    breaks = -2:2,           # Log10 breakpoints: 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 km/yr
    labels = c("<0.01", "0.1", "1", "10", ">100"),
    name = "Displacement [km/yr]",
    limit = c(-2, 2),        # Clip colour scale to 0.01–100 km/yr range
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = 15,
      barheight      = 0.5
    )
  ) +
  
  # Overlay country borders (transparent fill, outline only)
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +
  
  labs(
    title    = "Climate Velocity for 2100 under RCP 6.0",
    subtitle = "Seasonal climate velocity under RCP 6.0 (CCSM4) · Eckert IV equal-area projection"
  ) +
  
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )
```

Displacement is quantified as the geometric mean of the speeds of climate velocity vectors (@fig-Displace). In [@ordonez2024], this is based on the median of seasonal temperature and precipitation values (means/totals, respectively). This metric jointly captures the spatial gradient of climatic conditions and the temporal trajectory of climate change. Displacement thus reflects the rate at which species must shift their ranges to remain aligned with a changing climate. For instance, between 1901 and 2013, the global mean displacement for temperature and precipitation was estimated at 3.7 kilometers per decade [@ordonez2016], indicating the approximate rate at which species in a community must move to maintain cohesion.

```{r}
#| label: VelDisp_estimation
#| eval: false
# Displacement formula (Ordóñez et al. 2016):
#   For each season s:
#     V_T_s  = ΔT_s / ∇T_s     [km/yr] — scalar velocity for temperature
#     V_P_s  = ΔP_s / ∇P_s     [km/yr] — scalar velocity for precipitation
#     D_s    = sqrt(V_T_s² + V_P_s²)    — displacement magnitude for season s
#
#   Overall displacement:
#     D_total = geometric mean of D_s across all seasons
#             = 10^(mean(log10(D_s)))
#
#   Note: the geometric mean (via log-space averaging) is used rather than the
#   arithmetic mean because velocity/displacement distributions are strongly
#   right-skewed and span several orders of magnitude.
#
# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: COMPUTE CLIMATE DISPLACEMENT (SENSU ORDÓÑEZ ET AL. 2016)
# ==============================================================================
#
# Overall displacement across seasons uses the geometric mean (log-space mean)
# to account for the right-skewed, multi-order-of-magnitude distribution of
# displacement values across the globe.

# --- 1a. Load the Climate velocity vectors-------------------------------------
clim_vel_2100 <- rast("./Displacement/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_1970_to_2000_Velocity.Seasonal.tif")

# --- 1b. Compute per-season displacement: Euclidean norm of T and P velocity ve
seasons <- c("MAM", "JJA", "SON", "DJF")

Disp_seasonal <- lapply(seasons, function(s) {
  V_T <- clim_vel_2100[[paste0("Temp_", s)]]   # Temperature scalar velocity [km/yr]
  V_P <- clim_vel_2100[[paste0("Prec_", s)]]   # Precipitation scalar velocity [km/yr]
  sqrt(V_T^2 + V_P^2)  # Pythagorean combination — displacement for season s [km/yr]
})
names(Disp_seasonal) <- seasons

# Stack seasonal displacement layers
Disp_seasonal_stack <- Disp_seasonal[[1]]
for (i in 2:length(Disp_seasonal)){
  Disp_seasonal_stack <- c(Disp_seasonal_stack,Disp_seasonal[[i]])
}
names(Disp_seasonal_stack) <- seasons

# Geometric mean across seasons using log-space arithmetic mean
# Geometric mean = 10^(mean(log10(x))) — appropriate for multiplicative, 
# log-normally distributed quantities such as climate velocities

Disp_2100 <- 10^(mean(log10(Disp_seasonal_stack)))
names(Disp_2100) <- "Displacement_kmyr"

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: MAP CLIMATE DISPLACEMENT
# ==============================================================================

# --- 2a. Load medium-resolution country polygons and project to Eckert IV------
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")

# --- 2b. Plot------------------------------------------------------------------

ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +

  # Raster layer: log10-transformed displacement for display
  # log10 transformation linearises the colour scale across orders of magnitude
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(Disp_2100)) +

  # Colour scale with interpretable legend labels in original units (km/yr)
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",       # Muted, perceptually uniform palette
    na.value = NA,           # Transparent where data are missing (ocean, ice, etc.)
    breaks = -1:3,           # Log10 breakpoints: 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 km/yr
    labels = c("<0.1", "1", "10", "100",">1000"),
    name = "Displacement [km/yr]",
    limit = c(-1, 3),        # Clip colour scale to 0.01–100 km/yr range
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = 15,
      barheight      = 0.5
    )
  ) +

  # Overlay country borders (transparent fill, outline only)
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +

  labs(
    title    = "Climate Displacement for 2100 under RCP 6.0",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Displacement = geometric mean across seasons of √(V²_temp + V²_precip)\n",
      "Sensu Ordóñez et al. (2016, Nature Climate Change)"
    )
  ) +

  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-Displace
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Estimate of the displacement speed (in km per year) of seasonal climate variables between 2000 and 2100 under RCP 6.0.

# Load a single layer (third layer) from a multi-layer displacement raster
Disp_2100 <- rast("./Displacement/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_DispDiv_RCP60_1999to2049.tif")
Disp_2100 <- app(Disp_2100,function(x){ifelse(x>100,100,x)})

# --- 1a. Prepare basemap vector data ------------------------------------------

# Load medium-resolution country polygons and project to Eckert IV
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")

# --- 1b. Plot -----------------------------------------------------------------

# --- 2b. Plot------------------------------------------------------------------

ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +

  # Raster layer: log10-transformed displacement for display
  # log10 transformation linearises the colour scale across orders of magnitude
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(Disp_2100)) +

  # Colour scale with interpretable legend labels in original units (km/yr)
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",       # Muted, perceptually uniform palette
    na.value = NA,           # Transparent where data are missing (ocean, ice, etc.)
    breaks = -1:3,           # Log10 breakpoints: 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 km/yr
    labels = c("<0.1", "1", "10", "100",">1000"),
    name = "Displacement [km/yr]",
    limit = c(-1, 3),        # Clip colour scale to 0.01–100 km/yr range
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = 15,
      barheight      = 0.5
    )
  ) +

  # Overlay country borders (transparent fill, outline only)
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +

  labs(
    title    = "Climate Displacement for 2100 under RCP 6.0",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Displacement = geometric mean across seasons of √(V²_temp + V²_precip)\n",
      "Sensu Ordóñez et al. (2016, Nature Climate Change)"
    )
  ) +

  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

```

To illustrate this, we use the maximum dispersal distances reported by [@bullock2017] for 10 growth forms (herb, graminoid, shrub, tree) and different dispersal modes (ant, ballistic, wind, vertebrate, rodent), estimated from the best-fitting dispersal kernel functions (see @tbl-Bullock). We then compare these values with European climate displacement maps for 2100 under RCP 6.0 from [@ordonez2024]. For this, we extract the maximum distance that seeds from different plants can travel per reproductive event from [@bullock2017]. The Exponential Power and log-sech functions are the two best-performing models for describing seed dispersal kernels across a range of plant types and dispersal modes (@tbl-Bullock). Here, we focus on the long-distance dispersal events estimated by [@bullock2017].

| Growth Form | Dispersal Mode        | Exponential Power | Log-sec |
|-------------|-----------------------|:-----------------:|:-------:|
| Herb        | Ant                   |       1.552       |  1.572  |
| Herb        | Ballistic             |       4.926       |  6.838  |
| Herb        | Wind (with appendage) |       1.303       |  1.305  |
| Herb        | Wind (no appendage)   |       0.258       |  1.757  |
| Graminoid   | Wind (no appendage)   |       7.836       |  3.623  |
| Shrub       | Ant                   |       12.95       |  17.91  |
| Shrub       | Vertebrate            |       130.1       |  20.29  |
| Tree        | Rodent                |       245.2       |  35.12  |
| Tree        | Vertebrate            |       1751        |  897.1  |
| Tree        | Wind (with appendage) |       371.6       |  317.9  |

: Estimates of the maximum (95th percentile) distance (in m) seeds from species from four growth forms (i.e., Herb, Graminoid, Shrub, Tree) could move based on kernel-density estimates from [@bullock2017]. Values were estimated using both an exponential power ($\frac{b}{2 \pi a^2 \Gamma(2/b)} \exp\left(-\left(\frac{d}{a}\right)^b\right)$) and a log-hyperbolic secant function ($H(t) = a_{ij} x^{b_{ij}}$), as these distributions allow for the description of fat-tailed distributions.

Using these values, we estimate dispersal lag from the seed dispersal kernels reported in [@bullock2017]. Specifically, we compute an environmental (log) ratio between climate displacement and the potential seed movement associated with a given dispersal vector in a particular growth environment. This ratio contrasts the rate of climate displacement with the movement capacity of plant species, indicating whether climate change proceeds at a pace that species can track. As illustrated in @fig-KernelRto, a species group with a specific growth form and dispersal vector can either move faster than shifting climates (ratio \> 1 or positive log-ratio) or lag behind them (ratio \< 1 or negative log-ratio). As our purpose is to illustrate the aproach, we restrict our calculations to estimates derived from the Exponential Power function.

```{r}
#| label: fig-KernelRto
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Estimate of the difference between the maximum distance a seed can be dispersed per year and the displacement of seasonal climate variables per year from 2000 to 2100 under RCP 6.0.
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 8

# This section creates a multi-panel map visualization comparing species displacement 
# rates to climate velocity across different Growth form/vector combinations where:
##    - Red areas: Species displacement slower than climate velocity
##    - Neutral colors: Species displacement matches climate velocity  
##    - Blue areas: Species displacement faster than climate velocity
# - Each panel represents a different Growth form/vector combination from the input raster

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: MULTI PANEL VISUALIZATION
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Load medium-resolution country polygons and project to Eckert IV -------
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")

#--- 1b. Load a single layer (third layer) from a multi-layer displacement raster -
Disp_2100 <- rast("./Displacement/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_DispDiv_RCP60_1999to2049.tif")

#--- 1c. Load a table with the Movement capacity of different species. -----------
# This reads dispersal distance data from Bullock et al. 2017 study
MaxDispKer_Table <- read.csv("./Displacement/DisplacementRates/Bullock2017_Dispersal_Distances.csv")

#--- 1d. Create a combined variable ----------------------------------------------
# This combines Growth Form (e.g., tree, shrub, herb) and Dispersal Mode (e.g., wind, animal)
# into a single categorical variable for easier analysis and visualization
MaxDispKer_Table <- MaxDispKer_Table %>%
  mutate(Combined = str_c(`Growth.Form`, `Dispersal.Mode`, sep = "-"))

#--- 1e. Estimate the Log10 Ratio between the Maximum Movement capacity and climate -- displacement 
# This calculates how fast species can move relative to how fast climate is changing
##    - Exponential.Power/1000: converts dispersal distance to km (assuming original units in meters)
##    - Disp_2100: projected climate displacement distance by 2100 (defined elsewhere)
##    - log10 transformation: makes ratios easier to interpret (0 = equal rates, positive = faster than climate)
LogRto_DisplacemnetVsKernelDist <- log10((MaxDispKer_Table$Exponential.Power/1000)/Disp_2100)

# Assign meaningful names to the ratio values using the combined categories
names(LogRto_DisplacemnetVsKernelDist) <- MaxDispKer_Table$Combined


#--- 1f. plotting ---------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +                          
  # Initiate plot with the vector world map as base layer
  geom_spatraster(data = LogRto_DisplacemnetVsKernelDist) +   
  # Add raster layer containing log10-transformed displacement ratios
  # This shows how species displacement compares to climate velocity
  
  # Configure colour scale for continuous raster values
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",           # Use a muted colour palette for readability
    direction = -1,              # Reverse palette direction so red indicates lower values 
                                # (species moving slower than climate)
    na.value = NA,               # Do not assign a colour to NA values (keeps them transparent)
    breaks = c(-6, 0, 6),        # Legend breaks corresponding to log10 values
                                # -6 = 1/1,000,000x slower, 0 = same speed, 6 = 1,000,000x faster
    labels = c("Slower than\nClimate", "Same", "Faster than\nClimate"), 
                                # Human-readable labels that convert log10 back to linear interpretation
    name = "Ratio",              # Legend title indicating what the values represent
    limit = c(-6, 6),            # Limit the colour scale to show ratios from 6 orders of magnitude
                                # slower to 6 orders faster than climate velocity
    guide = guide_colorbar(      # Customize the color bar appearance
      title.position = "top",    # Position legend title above the color bar
      title.hjust = 0.5,         # Center the legend title horizontally
      barwidth = 15,             # Make color bar wider for better visibility
      barheight = 0.5            # Keep color bar relatively thin to save space
    )
  ) +
  
  facet_wrap(~lyr, ncol = 3) +   # Create multiple panels per layer in the raster
                                # Arranged in 3 columns for optimal layout
                                # Each panel shows a different dispersal type
  
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Overlay the world vector borders on top
                                # fill = NA means only outlines are drawn (no country fill)
  
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") + # Move the legend to the bottom for better layout
                                    # This maximizes space for the map panels
    # Add title and improve overall appearance
  labs(
    title    = "Match between climate displacement\nand dispersal capacity depending on dispersal vector",
    subtitle = "Log₁₀ ratio of seed dispersal distance per year to climate displacement rate (Bullock et al. 2017)"
  ) +
  
  # Adjust overall plot layout
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 2, 5, 2)
  )


```

We can extend this approach to quantify the mean dispersal lag across multiple groups. For each group, we compute the (log) ratio of climate displacement to seed dispersal distance and then take the harmonic mean of these ratios as a summary metric of dispersal lag. As illustrated in @fig-CommLag, this community-level metric is interpreted analogously to group-specific ratios but represents the aggregate dispersal lag of the assemblage.

```{r}
#| label: fig-CommLag
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Estimate the community-level lag, defined as the geometric mean of seed dispersal lag across 10 functional groups. For each group, differences are calculated as the ratio of the maximum distance a seed can be dispersed annually to the annual displacement of seasonal climate variables from 2000 to 2100 under RCP 6.0.


# This section creates a single-panel map visualization indicating the overall mismatch 
# between displacement rates to climate velocity summarized in the mismatch across
# different Growth form/vector combinations where:
##    - Values < 0 (red/warm colors): Species move slower than climate displacement
##    - Values = 0 (neutral): Species movement matches climate displacement  
##    - Values > 0 (blue/cool colors): Species move faster than climate displacement

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: SINGLE PANEL VISUALIZATION
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Load medium-resolution country polygons and project to Eckert IV -------
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")

#--- 1b. Calculate as the geometric mean of all species group ratios ------------
# This provides an overall measure of how well the biological community can track climate change
CommunityLag <- mean(LogRto_DisplacemnetVsKernelDist) 

#--- 1c. plotting ---------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +                          
  # Initiate plot with the vector world map as base layer
  geom_spatraster(data = CommunityLag) +   
  # Add raster layer showing movement capacity ratios across geographic space
  
    # Configure colour scale for continuous raster
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",           # Use muted color palette for better visual readability
    direction = -1,              # Reverse palette direction so red indicates lower values 
                                 # (species moving slower than climate)
    na.value = NA,               # Set NA values to transparent (no color assigned)
    breaks = c(-5,-2.5,0),          # Define legend breakpoints: 6 orders of magnitude range
    #labels = c("Slower than\nClimate", "Same", "Faster than\nClimate"), # User-friendly labels
    labels = c("Much slower than\nClimate",
               "Sliglty slower than\nClimate",
               "Same"), # User-friendly labels
    name = "Ratio",              # Title for the legend
    limit = c(-5, 0),            # Constrain color scale to ±6 orders of magnitude
    
    guide = guide_colorbar(      # Configure color bar appearance
      title.position = "top",    # Place legend title above the color bar
      title.hjust = 0.5,         # Center the legend title horizontally
      barwidth = 15,             # Increase color bar width for better visibility
      barheight = 0.5            # Keep color bar thin to conserve vertical space
    )) +
    
# Add country/region boundaries on top
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Overlay world borders as transparent polygons (outline only)
  
  # Add title and improve overall appearance
  labs(
    title    = "Match between climate displacement\nand dispersal capacity across dispersal vectors",
    subtitle = "Geometric mean dispersal lag across growth-form × dispersal-vector combinations"
  ) +
  
  # Adjust overall plot layout
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Mapping Abiotic Filtering {#sec-abiotic}

Mapping the role of abiotic filtering in the formation of novel ecosystems requires spatial assessments of environmental change, such as shifts in climate or land cover. This includes, for example, identifying areas with no-analogue states relative to historical baselines or evaluating changes in climatic suitability for community groups using species distribution models or stability metrics of species co-occurrence. These approaches help predict where abiotic filtering may exclude historically dominant species and favour those previously marginal or absent, recognising that no-analogue conditions can undermine traditional species–climate distribution predictions. To illustrate this, we map the emergence of novel climates by 2100 under RCP 6.0, following [@ordonez2024]. Here, novel climates refer to future climatic combinations with no current analogue (i.e. no location presently experiences climate conditions similar to those projected for that location in the future due to climate change; cf. [@williams2007; @jackson2000]).

Here, we build on the idea that no-analogue climates—future climate combinations with no close matches under current conditions—provide a useful basis for mapping the effects of environmental filtering. Following [@ordonez2024], we characterise no-analogue climates by estimating Climate Novelty for each grid cell in the future climate layer as the minimum Mahalanobis distance to any grid cell in the current climate space (@fig-Novelty). This measure captures how dissimilar future climate conditions are from those presently observed. The use of Mahalanobis distance is particularly advantageous because it incorporates correlations among climate variables, as shown by [@burke2019; @ordonez2024]. In contrast, other studies have relied on Standardized Euclidean Distances (SED), which measure Euclidean distance in climate space after standardising each dimension by the variable’s interannual variability (e.g. [@williams2007; @ordonez2014a]).

```{r}
#| label: NoveltyEstimate
#| eval: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# This script quantifies the degree to which future climates (2085–2115) lack
# analogues in the baseline climate (1970–2000), following the Mahalanobis
# distance framework of Ordóñez et al. (2016). Three outputs are stored per
# grid cell:
# Mahalanobis distance is used rather than Euclidean distance in climate space
# because it accounts for the covariance structure (and different units/scales)
# among climate variables, making the metric scale-independent.

# --- 0. Package loading ------------------------------------------------------

library(terra)              # Raster and vector spatial data handling
library(tidyterra)          # terra–ggplot2 integration
terraOptions(progress = 0)  # Suppress progress bars in rendered output
library(tidyverse)          # Data manipulation and visualisation
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth country polygons as sf objects
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Required data package for rnaturalearth
library(parallel)           # Base-R parallel computing (replaces deprecated snowfall)

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: LOAD AND PREPARE CLIMATE DATA
# ==============================================================================

# --- 1a. Baseline climate (historical: ~1970–2000, CCSM4)----------------------

# Layers 1–4: Seasonal mean temperature (°C) — MAM / JJA / SON / DJF
# Layers 5–8: Seasonal total precipitation (mm) — MAM / JJA / SON / DJF
clim_vars_now <- c(
  rast("./Novelty/Historical/CCSM4/CCSM4_1970_to_2000_Tm.Seasonal.tif"),
  rast("./Novelty/Historical/CCSM4/CCSM4_1970_to_2000_P.Seasonal.tif")
)
names(clim_vars_now) <- c(
  paste0("Temp_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF")),
  paste0("Prec_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF"))
)

# --- 1b. Future climate (2085–2115, RCP 6.0, CCSM4)----------------------------

clim_vars_fut <- c(
  rast("./Novelty/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_2085_to_2115_Tm.Seasonal.tif"),
  rast("./Novelty/RCP60/CCSM4/CCSM4_2085_to_2115_P.Seasonal.tif")
)
names(clim_vars_fut) <- c(
  paste0("Temp_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF")),
  paste0("Prec_", c("MAM","JJA","SON","DJF"))
)

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: REPROJECT AND RESAMPLE TO EQUAL-AREA GRID
# ==============================================================================
# Eckert IV (+proj=eck4) is an equal-area projection essential for ensuring
# that spatial gradient computations and geographic distances are in consistent,
# physically meaningful units (metres → km).

# --- 2a. Project both periods to Eckert IV-------------------------------------
StartPeriodPrj <- project(clim_vars_now, "+proj=eck4", method = "near", threads = 50)
EndPeriodPrj   <- project(clim_vars_fut, "+proj=eck4", method = "near", threads = 50)

# Template raster at 50 × 50 km resolution
# 50 km grain is appropriate for global analyses and avoids over-precision
# relative to the native GCM resolution (~100–200 km)
eck4Rast <- rast(
  extent     = ext(StartPeriodPrj),
  resolution = 50000,   # 50,000 m = 50 km
  crs        = "+proj=eck4"
)

# --- 2b. Resample to 50 × 50 km------------------------------------------------
# Temperature (layers 1–4): round to 1 decimal place (°C precision)
# Precipitation (layers 5–8): round to integer mm
StartPeriod_50x50 <- lapply(1:dim(StartPeriodPrj)[3], function(x) {
  round(resample(StartPeriodPrj[[x]], eck4Rast, threads = 50),
        ifelse(x >= 5, 0, 1))
})

EndPeriod_50x50 <- lapply(1:dim(EndPeriodPrj)[3], function(x) {
  round(resample(EndPeriodPrj[[x]], eck4Rast, threads = 50),
        ifelse(x >= 5, 0, 1))
})

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 3: PREPARE DATA FOR MAHALANOBIS DISTANCE COMPUTATION
# ==============================================================================

# Stack baseline (columns 1–8) and future (columns 9–16) into one value matrix.
# Rows with any NA (ocean, ice sheets, etc.) are removed so the covariance
# matrix and distance calculations are not distorted by missing data.
Normal_Future_Climate_rast <- values(
  c(do.call("c", StartPeriod_50x50),
    do.call("c", EndPeriod_50x50)),
  na.rm = FALSE   # Retain all rows; NAs filtered explicitly below
)

# --- 3a. Identify cells that have complete data in BOTH baseline and future per
# Only these cells enter the novelty computation; results are mapped back using
# their row indices (cell_with_data) at the end.
cell_with_data <- which(
  complete.cases(Normal_Future_Climate_rast)
)

# --- 3b. Subset to complete cases only-----------------------------------------
Normal_Future_Climate_rast <- Normal_Future_Climate_rast[cell_with_data, ]

# Scale the full matrix (baseline + future combined).
# Scaling to zero mean and unit variance is required before computing
# Mahalanobis distance to avoid variables with large absolute ranges (e.g.
# precipitation in mm) dominating over variables with small ranges (e.g.
# temperature in °C). Scaling is applied jointly so that future values are
# expressed in the same standardised units as the baseline reference.
Normal_Future_Climate_rastScl <- scale(Normal_Future_Climate_rast)

# Separate the scaled baseline (columns 1–8) and future (columns 9–16)
Baseline_Scl <- Normal_Future_Climate_rastScl[, 1:8]   # n_cells × 8
Future_Scl   <- Normal_Future_Climate_rastScl[, 9:16]  # n_cells × 8

# Pre-compute the covariance matrix of the BASELINE climate space.
# This is the reference distribution against which novelty is measured.
# Using only baseline data ensures the covariance structure reflects the
# observed historical climate, not the (potentially novel) future climate.
CoVarMtrx <- cov(Baseline_Scl)

# Retrieve the (projected, equal-area) coordinates for all complete cells.
# These are used to compute geographic distances between a future cell and
# its closest baseline analogue.
CordsNormal <- crds(StartPeriod_50x50[[1]], df = FALSE)[cell_with_data, ]


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 4: COMPUTE CLIMATE NOVELTY (PARALLEL)
# ==============================================================================
# For each future grid cell x:
#   (a) Extract the 8-variable future climate vector for cell x.
#   (b) Compute the Mahalanobis distance from cell x to every cell in the
#       baseline climate data frame (using the baseline covariance matrix).
#   (c) Identify the baseline cell with the minimum Mahalanobis distance
#       (= closest climatic analogue).
#   (d) If multiple cells share the minimum MD (ties), select the one that is
#       geographically closest to cell x.
#   (e) Record: MD.min, analogue cell index, geographic distance to analogue.
#
# Note: `parallel::mclapply` replaces the deprecated `snowfall::sfLapply`.
# On Windows, mclapply falls back to single-core; use parLapply instead.
# The number of cores (20) should be adjusted to the available hardware.

n_cores <- 20  # Adjust to available CPUs

MDMin_list <- mclapply(
  1:nrow(Future_Scl),
  function(x) {

    # (a) Future climate vector for cell x (1 × 8 named numeric vector)
    TrgCellVals <- Future_Scl[x, ]

    # (b) Mahalanobis distance from cell x to ALL baseline cells
    # mahalanobis() returns a vector of length n_baseline_cells
    MD_all <- mahalanobis(Baseline_Scl,
                          center = TrgCellVals,
                          cov    = CoVarMtrx)

    # (c) Index / indices of the closest baseline analogue(s)
    min_MD    <- min(MD_all, na.rm = TRUE)
    tied_idx  <- which(MD_all == min_MD)   # may be >1 if tied

    # (d) Among tied analogues, select the geographically nearest one
    if (length(tied_idx) == 1) {
      best_idx <- tied_idx
    } else {
      geo_dists <- as.vector(
        terra::distance(
          x      = matrix(CordsNormal[x, ],       ncol = 2),
          y      = matrix(CordsNormal[tied_idx, ], ncol = 2),
          lonlat = FALSE          # coordinates are in metres (Eckert IV)
        )
      )
      best_idx <- tied_idx[which.min(geo_dists)]
    }

    # (e) Geographic distance (km) from future cell x to its closest analogue
    DistKm <- as.vector(
      terra::distance(
        x      = matrix(CordsNormal[x, ],        ncol = 2),
        y      = matrix(CordsNormal[best_idx, ], ncol = 2),
        lonlat = FALSE
      )
    ) / 1000   # convert metres → km

    # Return a one-row data frame for this cell
    data.frame(
      ID       = x,
      MD.min   = round(min_MD, 3),  # Minimum Mahalanobis distance (novelty degree)
      Cell     = best_idx,           # Baseline row index of closest analogue
      DistinKm = round(DistKm, 2)   # Geographic distance to closest analogue (km)
    )
  },
  mc.cores = n_cores
)

# --- 4a. Combine per-cell results into a single data frame---------------------
MDMin <- do.call("rbind", MDMin_list)

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 5: RECONSTRUCT RASTER FROM RESULTS
# ==============================================================================
# --- 5a. Assign the three output metrics back to a spatial raster that matches 
# 50 × 50 km Eckert IV grid. Only cells in cell_with_data receive values;
# all other cells (ocean, no-data) remain NA.

# Initialise a three-layer raster with the same extent/CRS as the working grid
MDminSumm <- rast(eck4Rast, nlyrs = 3)

# --- 5b. Write results into the rows corresponding to complete-data cells------
# MDMin[, -1] drops the ID column, leaving MD.min / Cell / DistinKm
MDminSumm[cell_with_data] <- as.matrix(MDMin[, -1])

names(MDminSumm) <- c("MD.min", "Cell", "DistinKm")

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 6: MAP CLIMATE NOVELTY
# ==============================================================================

# --- 6a. Load and project country boundaries to Eckert IV for overlay----------
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")

# Log10-transform MD.min for display: the raw Mahalanobis distance spans several
# orders of magnitude globally, so a log scale provides a better-balanced map.
# Legend breaks are set at meaningful quantile-derived thresholds.
log10_breaks <- c(-2.097, -1.330, -0.446,  0.675,  1.429)
break_labels <- c("Min", "Low", "Medium", "High", "Maximum")

ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +

  # Raster layer: log10(MD.min) — layer 1 of MDminSumm
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(MDminSumm[["MD.min"]])) +

  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,
    breaks   = log10_breaks,
    labels   = break_labels,
    name     = "Climate novelty (Mahalanobis distance)",
    guide    = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = 15,
      barheight      = 0.5
    )
  ) +

  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Country borders, outline only

  labs(
    title    = "Degree of climate novelty by 2100 under RCP 6.0",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Novelty = minimum Mahalanobis distance of future climate to any baseline cell\n",
      "Sensu Ordóñez et al. (2016, Nature Climate Change)"
    )
  ) +

  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-Novelty
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Climate novelty assessed by computing the minimum Mahalanobis distance between future climate conditions and the range of present climate variability, enabling the identification of areas where future climates will differ significantly from current conditions. The output indicates the minimum Mahalanobis distance from a future climate condition to the nearest present climate analog across all current locations.
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

# --- 1a. Package loading ------------------------------------------------------

library(terra)              # Raster and vector spatial data handling
library(tidyterra)          # terra–ggplot2 integration
terraOptions(progress = 0)  # Suppress progress bars in rendered output
library(tidyverse)          # Data manipulation and visualisation
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth country polygons as sf objects
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Required data package for rnaturalearth
library(parallel)           # Base-R parallel computing (replaces deprecated snowfall)


#--- 1b.Data Loading --------------------------------------------------
# Load pre-computed climate novelty raster based on Mahalanobis distance
# File contains minimum Mahalanobis distance values for RCP 6.0 scenario (2099)
MDminSumm <- rast("./Novelty/RCP60/CCSM4/MDminSumm_CCSM4_RCP60_2100.tif",
                     lyrs=1)

#--- 1c. Load and project country boundaries to Eckert IV for overlay -
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")

#--- 1d. Log10-transform MD.min for display: the raw Mahalanobis distance spans several
# orders of magnitude globally, so a log scale provides a better-balanced map.
# Legend breaks are set at meaningful quantile-derived thresholds.
log10_breaks <- c(-2.097, -1.330, -0.446,  0.675,  1.429)
break_labels <- c("Min", "Low", "Medium", "High", "Maximum")

#--- 1b.Ploting ------------------------------------------------------ 
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +

  # Raster layer: log10(MD.min) — layer 1 of MDminSumm
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(MDminSumm[["MD.min"]])) +

  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,
    breaks   = log10_breaks,
    labels   = break_labels,
    name     = "Climate novelty (Mahalanobis distance)",
    guide    = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = 15,
      barheight      = 0.5
    )
  ) +

  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Country borders, outline only

  labs(
    title    = "Degree of climate novelty by 2100 under RCP 6.0",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Novelty = minimum Mahalanobis distance of future climate to any baseline cell\n",
      "Sensu Ordóñez et al. (2016, Nature Climate Change)"
    )
  ) +

  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Mapping Biotic Interactions {#sec-biotic}

Biotic interactions are central to the formation of novel ecosystems because they shape community assembly, demographic dynamics, and species coexistence. Processes such as competition, predation, mutualism, and parasitism determine where species can establish and persist, and thereby structure emerging communities. By influencing selection and local adaptation, shifts in interaction networks can trigger eco-evolutionary feedbacks, reinforcing priority effects and creating path-dependent trajectories of change. These cascading outcomes are pivotal for the emergence of novel ecosystems: as species assemblages reorganise under environmental change, restructured interaction networks can decouple communities from historical baselines and generate unprecedented species combinations. Mapping biotic interaction dynamics provides a critical lens for anticipating where and how novel ecological states are likely to arise.

One simple way to visualise potential changes in species interactions is to map shifts in seed-disperser diversity resulting from the loss of all currently endangered seed-dispersing species. To do this, we use data from [@fricke2022] to quantify the global diversity of seed dispersers (birds and mammals; @fig-DispRich) and the diversity of those species classified as endangered, based on IUCN categories Vulnerable, Endangered, Critically Endangered, Extinct in the Wild, and Extinct (@fig-DispEndg). We then calculate the proportion of current species richness that is at risk (@fig-DispRiskChng), thereby identifying areas where a high fraction of mutualistic interactions is likely to be lost.

Here, we use distribution data for seed-dispersing vertebrates—birds and mammals—originally compiled and employed by @fricke2022. Mammalian range estimates were drawn from the Phylogenetic Atlas of Mammal Macroecology, PHYLACINE 1.2.1 [@faurby2020], a comprehensive, globally standardised database that provides both present-natural and current range maps for all extant and recently extinct mammal species, incorporating phylogenetic information alongside spatial range data. This dataset is particularly well-suited for analyses of biotic interactions under environmental change, as it explicitly distinguishes between ranges reflecting species' ecological potential and those shaped by contemporary human impacts such as hunting and habitat loss. Bird ranges were sourced from BirdLife International [@BirdLifeMaps2024], the globally recognised authority on avian distribution, whose spatial range data are compiled from expert knowledge, field surveys, and remote sensing, and are updated periodically to reflect range contractions, expansions, and extinction events.

```{r}
#| label: fig-DispRich
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Global map showing the richness of plant seed dispersers (birds and mammals) -  Data from [@fricke2022].
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8


# --- 0. Package loading ------------------------------------------------------

library(terra)              # Raster and vector spatial data handling
library(tidyterra)          # terra–ggplot2 integration
terraOptions(progress = 0)  # Suppress progress bars in rendered output
library(tidyverse)          # Data manipulation and visualisation
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth country polygons as sf objects
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Required data package for rnaturalearth
library(parallel)           # Base-R parallel computing (replaces deprecated snowfall)


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: MAP THE DISTRIBUTION OF SEED DISPERSERS
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Load the distribution maps --------------------------------------------
#### Load mammals distribution maps
mamm_pres <- rast("./Interactions/Dispersers/rast_mamm_pres_nat.tif")
#### Load birds distribution maps
bird_pres <- rast("./Interactions/Dispersers/rast_bird_pres_nat.tif")
#### make a spatRast for All Animals
animal_pres <- c(mamm_pres,bird_pres)

#--- 1b. Define Disperser richness ---------------------------------------------
disperser.rich.Rast <- sum(animal_pres)

#--- 1c. Load and project country boundaries to Eckert IV for overlay ----------
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")


#--- 1d. plotting --------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +                          # Initiate plot with the vector world map as base
  # wrld_simpl2 is a spatial vector data-set (likely country boundaries)
  # This establishes the coordinate system and base layer for the map
  
  geom_spatraster(data = disperser.rich.Rast) +
  # geom_spatraster() plots raster data (gridded spatial data)
  
  # Configure colour scale for continuous raster
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",           # Use a muted colour palette for readability
    # "muted" creates softer, less saturated colors that are easier on the eyes
    # white-box package provides scientifically-appropriate color palettes
    
    na.value = NA,               # Do not assign a colour to NA values
    # NA values (missing data) will be transparent/invisible on the map
    # This prevents misleading visualization of missing data areas
    
    breaks = seq(0,1200,by=120),               # Legend breaks
    labels = seq(0,1200,by=120), # Human readable labels
    
    name = "Disperser fauna richness",     # Legend title indicating the unit
  
      limit = c(0, 1200),            # Limit the colour scale to 0 and 1
    # Values outside this range will be colored as the extreme colors
    # This prevents outliers from dominating the color scale
    
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",  # Position the legend title above the color bar
      title.hjust = 0.5,       # Center the title horizontally (0.5 = center)
      barwidth = 15,           # Make the color bar 15 units wide (relatively wide)
      barheight = 0.5          # Make the color bar 0.5 units tall (relatively thin)
    )
  ) +
  
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Overlay the world vector borders on top without fill (only outlines)
  # This adds country/region boundaries as black lines
  # fill = NA means no fill color, just the outline
  # This helps viewers identify geographic regions
  
  labs(
    title    = "Current diversity of seed dispersers",
    subtitle = "Birds and mammals combined · Data from Fricke et al. (2022)"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )

```

For both taxonomic groups, range maps were intersected with occurrence data from the IUCN Red List, which provides additional information on conservation status and population trends. Together, distribution data from PHYLACINE/BirdLife and IUCN offer broad taxonomic coverage of functionally important seed-dispersing vertebrates and provide a robust empirical basis for assessing how changes in vertebrate distributions disrupt plant–animal mutualistic networks and, ultimately, drive shifts in community composition and ecological novelty. To explore this last point, we considered a scenario of changing contemporary mammal and bird species composition. Specifically, we modelled an extinction scenario in which all extant animal species currently listed as Vulnerable, Endangered, Critically Endangered, Extinct in the Wild, or Extinct in the IUCN Red List are hypothetically lost, while retaining the present ranges of all species not classified under these categories (@fig-DispEndg). For this, we relied on the endangerment scenarios compiled by @fricke2022.

```{r}
#| label: fig-DispEndg
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Global map showing the richness plant seed dispersers (birds and mammals) considered at risk of extinction (species assessed as Critically Endangered, Endangered, or Vulnerable by the IUCN – REF). Data from [@fricke2022].
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: DEFINE CONSERVATION STATUS
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Load Trait data for dispersers ---------------------------------------------
## Load the bird trait data from Fricke
load("./Interactions/Dispersers/all.bird.traits_Simple.RData") #--> traits birds
#### Filter to the variables of interest
all.bird.traits <- all.bird.traits[,c("name.m","animal.group","iucn.category")]

## Load the mammal trait data from Fricke
load("./Interactions/Dispersers/all.mamm.traits_Simple.RData") #--> traits mammals
#### Filter to the variables of interest
all.mamm.traits <- all.mamm.traits[,c("name.m","animal.group","iucn.category")]
#### Full Animal Matrix
all.animal.traits <- rbind(all.bird.traits,all.mamm.traits)


## Match the trait matrix to the occurrence raster
all.animal.traits.filetered <- all.animal.traits[gsub(" ",".",all.animal.traits$name.m)%in%names(animal_pres),]

#--- 1b. Create a 1/0 Vector saying if a species is Endangered or not ----------
## Here the Vulnerable, Endangered, Critically Endangered, Extinct in the Wild and Extinct cathetegories are used to define "Risk".
is.at.risk <- (all.animal.traits.filetered$iucn.category%in%c("EN","VU","CR","CR (PE)","EP","EX","EW"))*1
names(is.at.risk) <- gsub(" ",".",all.animal.traits.filetered$name.m) 
## Reorganize so the risk vector is in the same order as the names in the raster file
is.at.risk <- is.at.risk[names(animal_pres)]


#--- 1c. Estimate the richness of endangered dispersers ------------------------
## Set the presence value to 0 for all species that are considered endangered.
endangered.presence.Rast <- animal_pres[[is.at.risk==T]]
## Estimate the richness of endangered dispersers
endangered.rich.Rast <- sum(endangered.presence.Rast)

#--- 1d. Load and project country boundaries to Eckert IV for overlay ----------
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")

#--- 1e. plotting --------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +                          # Initiate plot with the vector world map as base
  geom_spatraster(data = endangered.rich.Rast) +   

  # Configure colour scale for continuous raster
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",           # Use a muted colour palette for readability
    na.value = NA,               # Do not assign a colour to NA values
    # NA values (missing data) will be transparent/invisible on the map
    breaks = seq(0,70,by=10),               # Legend breaks
    labels = seq(0,70,by=10), # Human readable labels
    name = "Endangered disperser fauna richness",     # Legend title indicating the unit
    
      limit = c(0, 70),            # Limit the colour scale to 0 and 1
    # Values outside this range will be colored as the extreme colors
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",  # Position the legend title above the color bar
      title.hjust = 0.5,       # Center the title horizontally (0.5 = center)
      barwidth = 15,           # Make the color bar 15 units wide (relatively wide)
      barheight = 0.5          # Make the color bar 0.5 units tall (relatively thin)
    )
  ) +
  
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Overlay the world vector borders on top without fill (only outlines)
  
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") + # Move the legend to the bottom for better layout
  
  labs(
    title    = "Current diversity of endangered seed dispersers",
    subtitle = "Species assessed as at risk by IUCN · Data from Fricke et al. (2022)"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

```

Using the distribution and endangerment information, we then estimate the proportion of seed-disperser species (birds and mammals) considered at risk of extinction (@fig-DispRiskChng). We calculate the ratio of the endangerment-reduced assemblage richness to the full current assemblage richness, expressed as $\text{Reduced} / \text{Full}$ as a metric of "Interaction novelty". This ratio ranges from zero, indicating that all locally present seed dispersers are at risk of extinction, to one, indicating that no species in the local assemblage face elevated extinction risk, and thus provides an intuitive, spatially explicit index of how much of the current seed-dispersal function is likely to be retained under ongoing biodiversity loss.

Using the distribution and endangerment information, we then estimate the proportion of seed-disperser species (birds and mammals) considered at risk of extinction (@fig-DispRiskChng). We calculate the ratio of the endangerment-reduced assemblage richness to the full current assemblage richness, expressed as $\text{Reduced} / \text{Full}$ as a metric of "Interaction novelty". This ratio ranges from zero, indicating that all locally present seed dispersers are at risk of extinction, to one, indicating that no species in the local assemblage face elevated extinction risk, and thus provides an intuitive, spatially explicit index of how much of the current seed-dispersal function is likely to be retained under ongoing biodiversity loss.

```{r}
#| label: fig-DispRiskChng
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Global map showing the proportion of plant seed dispersers (birds and mammals) considered at risk of extinction (species assessed as Critically Endangered, Endangered, or Vulnerable by the IUCN – REF). Data from [@fricke2022].
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: DEFINE PROPORTION OF DISPERSERS LOST
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Estimate the Diversity change
Disperser.rich.change.Rast <- (log10(endangered.rich.Rast/disperser.rich.Rast))

#--- 1b. Load and project country boundaries to Eckert IV for overlay ----------
world       <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf"))
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world, "+proj=eck4")


# 1c. plotting -----------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +                          # Initiate plot with the vector world map as base

  geom_spatraster(data = Disperser.rich.change.Rast) +   # Add raster layer after log10 transformation (e.g., for better scaling)
  # Configure colour scale for continuous raster
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",           # Use a muted colour palette for readability
    na.value = NA,               # Do not assign a colour to NA values
    breaks = c(0.025,log10(c(5,10,20,30,40)/100)),               # Legend breaks
    labels = c("<2%",paste0(c(5,10,20,30,40),"%")), # Human readable labels
    
    name = "Proportion of the disperser fauna\n clasified as endangered",     # Legend title indicating the unit
    
      limit = c(-1.6, -0.3),            # Limit the colour scale to -1.6 and -0.3 in log scale
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",  # Position the legend title above the color bar
      title.hjust = 0.5,       # Center the title horizontally (0.5 = center)
      barwidth = 15,           # Make the color bar 15 units wide (relatively wide)
      barheight = 0.5          # Make the color bar 0.5 units tall (relatively thin)
    )
  ) +
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Overlay the world vector borders on top without fill (only outlines)
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") + # Move the legend to the bottom for better layout
  labs(
    title = "Diversity of seed dispersers loss.",
    subtitle = "Proportion of the current diversity of seed dispersers\nlost if species classified as endangered go extinct."
  ) + 
  # Adjust overall plot layout
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Ecological Drift {#sec-drift}

Ecological drift (i.e., stochastic fluctuations in species abundances and occurrences [@vellend2010]) can be quantified by measuring the extent to which observed patterns of species composition and abundance reflect stochastic demographic processes rather than deterministic environmental or biotic filtering. The primary approach involves comparing observed communities against stochastic expectations generated through null models, which randomize species occurrences or abundances while maintaining key constraints such as species frequencies or total richness @Harvey1983; @ulrich2010; @gotelli1996null.

Here we use ranges, Extent of Occurrence endangerment estimates, and dated phylogeny information, to assess how the phylogeny diversity of plant assemblages (i.e., the sum of branch lengths of all the species in an Assemble - Faith Phylogeny diversity [@faith1992]) would change if all threatened plants (i.e., Vulnerable, Endangered, and Critically Endangered) were lost, and how this change compares to the loss of a random subset of the same number of species. This comparison allowed us to calculate the magnitude of ecological drift relative to deterministic assembly processes using the Standardised Effect Sizes (SES) as a metric, as it allows inference about the magnitude of drift relative to deterministic assembly processes.

Here, we leverage the iNaturalist Geomodel [@cole2023] used to develop the [iNaturalist Open Range Map Dataset](https://www.inaturalist.org/pages/range_maps) and focus on the 41,879 ranges generated for plant species. We use this data-set to illustrate other potential data sources that could be applied when estimating the mechanisms driving the emergence of ecological novelty. Also, we acknowledge that this is a very small subset of all plant species (\~350,000; sensu [@WCVP2024]), but it provides a large enough pool of species with range data, that can be easily downloaded, so we can demonstrate our ideas.

Range data for plant species are provided as polygon vectors in GeoPackage (`.gpkg`) format from the iNaturalist geomodel. Here we rasterise each species polygon to a common grid template. Each GeoPackage batch is processed separately to manage memory. Also, only records with `rank == "species"` are rasterised; higher-level taxa are skipped. Output rasters are written as multi-layer GeoTIFFs, one per batch. After rasterising all batches, we stack them and compute species richness (the number of species present in each grid cell) to see the patterns in the iNaturalist data-set (@fig-richness-map).

```{r}
#| label: load-vectors
#| eval: false

#--- 0. Load Packages ----------------------------------------------------------
library(terra)              # Raster and vector spatial data handling
library(tidyterra)          # terra–ggplot2 integration
terraOptions(progress = 0)  # Suppress progress bars in rendered output
library(tidyverse)          # Data manipulation and visualisation
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth country polygons as sf objects
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Required data for rnaturalearth
library(parallel)           # Run in parallel
library(sf)                 # load and work with sf objects 

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: MAP THE DISTRIBUTION OF PLANT SPECIES
# ==============================================================================


#--- 1a. Load all GeoPackages in the working directory into a list. ------------
# Each .gpkg may contain multiple records at different taxonomic ranks
# (species, genus, family, etc.), so we will filter to species-level later.
plants_ranges_vct_list <- lapply(
  dir("./Drift/Distributions/",
      pattern = ".gpkg",
      full.names = TRUE),
  function(x) st_read(x)
)

#--- 1b. Re-project Vectors ----------------------------------------------------

# Load the raster template that defines the target grid (extent,
# resolution, CRS). All rasters produced in this pipeline will share
# this grid. The template is derived from pre-existing plant presence data.
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]

# Re-project all range vectors to match the CRS of the raster template.
# This is necessary before rasterisation to ensure spatial alignment.
plants_ranges_vct_reproject_list <- lapply(
  plants_ranges_vct_list,
  function(x) {
    st_transform(x, crs = terra::crs(plant_rast_template, proj = TRUE))
  }
)

#--- 1c. Rasterise and Save the range maps -------------------------------------

for (j in seq_along(plants_ranges_vct_reproject_list)) {
  
  # Rasterise each species polygon to the template grid.
  # Returns NULL for non-species records (genus, family, etc.).
  plants_ranges_rast_list <- lapply(
    seq_len(nrow(plants_ranges_vct_reproject_list[[j]])),
    function(i) {
      vct_use <- plants_ranges_vct_reproject_list[[j]][i, ]
      
      if (vct_use$rank == "species") {
        r <- rasterize(vct_use[, "rank"], plant_rast_template)
        names(r) <- vct_use$name   # Use species name as layer name
        return(r)
      }
      # Implicitly returns NULL for non-species records
    }
  )
  
  # Combine species rasters into a single multi-layer raster,
  # dropping NULL entries from non-species records
  plants_ranges_rast <- do.call(
    "c",
    plants_ranges_rast_list[which(plants_ranges_rast_list != "NULL")]
  )
  
  # Save the batch raster
  writeRaster(
    plants_ranges_rast,
    paste0("./Drift/Distributions/iNaturalist_geomodel_Plantae_", j, ".tif"),
    overwrite = TRUE
  )
}

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: PLOT THE PLANT SPECIES RICHNESS
# ==============================================================================


#--- 2a. Sum layers to get species richness per cell; ignore NAs (ocean/no-data)
plant_richness <- app(plant_range_raster, sum, na.rm = TRUE)

#--- 2b. Mask out ocean cells using the template (which only covers land) ------
plant_richness_masked <- mask(plant_richness, plant_rast_template)

#--- 2c. Load and project global vector data
world <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")) 
# Retrieves country polygons at medium resolution and converts them to a 'SpatVector'
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world,"+proj=eck4")
# Projects the world map into Eckert IV projection, suitable for global thematic maps

#--- 2d. plotting --------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +                          # Initiate plot with the vector world map as base
                                               # wrld_simpl2 is a spatial vector data-set (likely country boundaries)
                                               # This establishes the coordinate system and base layer for the map
  
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(plant_richness_masked)) +   # Add raster layer after log10 transformation (e.g., for better scaling)
                                               # geom_spatraster() plots raster data (gridded spatial data)
                                               # Disp_2100 appears to be displacement data, possibly climate projections for year 2100
                                               # log10() transformation compresses the data range and makes extreme values more visible
                                               # This is common when data spans several orders of magnitude
  
  # Configure colour scale for continuous raster
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",           # Use a muted colour palette for readability
                                 # "muted" creates softer, less saturated colors that are easier on the eyes
                                 # white-box package provides scientifically-appropriate color palettes
    
    na.value = NA,               # Do not assign a colour to NA values
                                 # NA values (missing data) will be transparent/invisible on the map
                                 # This prevents misleading visualization of missing data areas
    
    breaks = 1:4,                # Legend breaks corresponding to log10 values
                                 # Creates 5 breaks: -2, -1, 0, 1, 2
                                 # These correspond to 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 in original units
    
    labels = 10^c(1:4),          # Labels convert log10 back to linear interpretation

    name = "Species Richness",   # Legend title indicating the unit
                                 # Clearly identifies what the colors represent
                                 # Units suggest this is measuring speed/rate of change
    
    limit = c(1, 4),            # Limit the colour scale to log10(10) and log10(10000)
                                 # Values outside this range will be colored as the extreme colors
                                 # This prevents outliers from dominating the color scale
    
    guide = guide_colorbar(
        title.position = "top",  # Position the legend title above the color bar
        title.hjust = 0.5,       # Center the title horizontally (0.5 = center)
        barwidth = 15,           # Make the color bar 15 units wide (relatively wide)
        barheight = 0.5          # Make the color bar 0.5 units tall (relatively thin)
    )
  ) +
  
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Overlay the world vector borders on top without fill (only outlines)
                                 # This adds country/region boundaries as black lines
                                 # fill = NA means no fill color, just the outline
                                 # This helps viewers identify geographic regions
  
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Species Richness",
    subtitle = "iNaturalist Geomodel ranges · log₁₀(species per cell) · Eckert IV equal-area projection"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-richness-map
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Species richness per grid cell across all plant species with iNaturalist range data.
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

library(terra)              # Raster and vector spatial data handling
library(tidyterra)          # terra–ggplot2 integration
terraOptions(progress = 0)  # Suppress progress bars in rendered output
library(tidyverse)          # Data manipulation and visualisation
library(rnaturalearth)      # Natural Earth country polygons as sf objects
library(rnaturalearthdata)  # Required data for rnaturalearth
library(parallel)           # Run in parallel
library(sf)                 # load and work with sf objects 

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: PLOT THE PLANT SPECIES RICHNESS
# ==============================================================================

#---1b. Load the Richness raster------------------------------------------------
plant_richness_masked <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/iNaturalist_richness.tif")

#---1b. Load and project global vector data ------------------------------------
world <- vect(ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")) 
# Retrieves country polygons at medium resolution and converts them to a 'SpatVector'
wrld_simpl2 <- project(world,"+proj=eck4")
# Projects the world map into Eckert IV projection, suitable for global thematic maps

#---1c. plotting ---------------------------------------------------------------
ggplot(wrld_simpl2) +                          # Initiate plot with the vector world map as base
                                               # wrld_simpl2 is a spatial vector data-set (likely country boundaries)
                                               # This establishes the coordinate system and base layer for the map
  
  geom_spatraster(data = log10(plant_richness_masked)) +   # Add raster layer after log10 transformation (e.g., for better scaling)
                                               # geom_spatraster() plots raster data (gridded spatial data)
                                               # Disp_2100 appears to be displacement data, possibly climate projections for year 2100
                                               # log10() transformation compresses the data range and makes extreme values more visible
                                               # This is common when data spans several orders of magnitude
  
  # Configure colour scale for continuous raster
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette = "muted",           # Use a muted colour palette for readability
                                 # "muted" creates softer, less saturated colors that are easier on the eyes
                                 # white-box package provides scientifically-appropriate color palettes
    
    na.value = NA,               # Do not assign a colour to NA values
                                 # NA values (missing data) will be transparent/invisible on the map
                                 # This prevents misleading visualization of missing data areas
    
    breaks = 1:4,                # Legend breaks corresponding to log10 values
                                 # Creates 5 breaks: -2, -1, 0, 1, 2
                                 # These correspond to 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 in original units
    
    labels = 10^c(1:4),          # Labels convert log10 back to linear interpretation

    name = "Species Richness",   # Legend title indicating the unit
                                 # Clearly identifies what the colors represent
                                 # Units suggest this is measuring speed/rate of change
```
