```{r}
    limit = c(1, 4),            # Limit the colour scale to log10(10) and log10(10000)
                                 # Values outside this range will be colored as the extreme colors
                                 # This prevents outliers from dominating the color scale
    
    guide = guide_colorbar(
        title.position = "top",  # Position the legend title above the color bar
        title.hjust = 0.5,       # Center the title horizontally (0.5 = center)
        barwidth = 15,           # Make the color bar 15 units wide (relatively wide)
        barheight = 0.5          # Make the color bar 0.5 units tall (relatively thin)
    )
  ) +
  
  geom_spatvector(fill = NA) +   # Overlay the world vector borders on top without fill (only outlines)
                                 # This adds country/region boundaries as black lines
                                 # fill = NA means no fill color, just the outline
                                 # This helps viewers identify geographic regions
  
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Species Richness",
    subtitle = "iNaturalist Geomodel ranges · log_10(species per cell)"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin      = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )
```

Using the iNaturalist ranges, we then estimated the Extent of Occurrence (EOO) of the evaluated species and used it to (i) define species assemblages at a 10 × 10 km resolution and (ii) estimate the likely IUCN endangerment category based on area (criterion B), as summarised in [@collen2016]. We approximate IUCN Red List threat categories using the **Extent of Occurrence (EOO)** thresholds defined by @collen2016:

| Category | EOO threshold |
|:--------:|:-------------:|
|    CR    | \< 1,000 km²  |
|    EN    | \< 5,000 km²  |
|    VU    | \< 20,000 km² |
|    NT    | ≥ 20,000 km²  |

```{r}
#| label: iucn-status
#| eval: false

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: DEFINE ENDENGERMENT STATUS 
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. For each GeoPackage, calculate the area of each species range polygon 
#        and classify it according to EOO-based IUCN thresholds. ---------------
IUCN_status_list <- lapply(
    dir("./Plants_Phylo",
      pattern = ".gpkg",
      full.names = TRUE),
  function(x) {
    
    Vect_Ranges <- st_read(x)
    
    # Keep only species-level records
    Vect_Ranges_spp <- Vect_Ranges[Vect_Ranges$rank == "species", ]
    
    IUCN_list <- lapply(
      seq_len(nrow(Vect_Ranges_spp)),
      function(z) {
        # Compute area in km² (st_area returns m²; divide by 1e6)
        area_km2 <- as.numeric(
          st_area(st_make_valid(Vect_Ranges_spp[z, ])) / 1e6
        )
        
        # Classify using IUCN EOO thresholds (Collen et al. 2016)
        status <- dplyr::case_when(
          area_km2 <  10000  ~ "CR",
          area_km2 <  50000  ~ "EN",
          area_km2 < 100000  ~ "VU",
          TRUE              ~ "NT"
        )
        
        data.frame(
          species  = Vect_Ranges_spp[z, ]$name,
          area.km2 = area_km2,
          status   = status
        )
      }
    )
    
    do.call("rbind", IUCN_list)
  }
)

# Combine all batches
IUCN_status_Tbl <- do.call("rbind", IUCN_status_list)

# Save
write.csv(IUCN_status_Tbl,
          "./Drift/IUCN/IUCN_status_Plants.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)

#1b. Count species per category, ordered CR → NT
IUCN_summary <- table(IUCN_status_Tbl$status)[c("CR", "EN", "VU", "NT")]

#1c. PLOT
barplot(IUCN_summary,
        log  = "y",
        las  = 1,
        col  = c("#d73027", "#f46d43", "#fdae61", "#74add1"),
        ylab = "Number of species (log scale)",
        main = "IUCN threat status (EOO-based)")

```

We then generated a phylogeny for these species using [`U.PhyloMaker`](https://github.com/jinyizju/U.PhyloMaker). As described in [@jin2019], to construct a dated phylogeny tree using `U.PhyloMaker` we will use a backbone tree (i.e., the GBOTB tree, which is a combination of [@smith2018] and [@zanne2013]), anchored to the World Checklist of Vascular Plants nomenclature [@WCVP2024]. As many of the evaluated species are not the backbone tree, we load a Genus–Family Table to help randomly grafting unknown species to the genus. The end phylogeny contains most of the species, noting that some species cannot be grafted because their genera do not match any in the backbone phylogeny. We visualise the phylogeny at Genus Level (@fig-plot-genus-phylogeny) by pruning it to one representative species per genus before plotting.

```{r}
#| label: extract-species-names
#| eval: false

#--- 0. Load the library -------------------------------------------------------
library(U.PhyloMaker)


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: BUILD A PHYLOGENY FOR EVALUATED SPECIES 
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1b. Load the date to create the phylogeny ---------------------------------

#Subset to species-rank names only 
spp_use <- names(rast("./Drift/Distributions/iNaturalist_geomodel_Plantae_ALL.tif"))

# Load the GBOTB mega-tree (backbone phylogeny for vascular plants). ----
# Source: http://sftp.kew.org/pub/data-repositories/WCVP/
megatree.Plants <- read.tree("./Drift/Phylogeny/GBOTB.extended.WCVP.tre")

# Load the genus-to-family look-up table required by U.PhyloMaker
gen.list.Plants <- read.csv("./Drift/Phylogeny/Genus_Family.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
names(gen.list.Plants) <- tolower(colnames(gen.list.Plants))

#--- 1c. Format species list: replace dots/underscores with spaces (common in
#        database exports), and extract genus as first word of species name ----
plant_for_phylo <- data.frame(
  species = gsub("[._]", " ", spp_use),
  genus   = sapply(strsplit(spp_use, "[ ]"), function(x) x[1])
)

# Remove records with malformed names (fewer than two space-separated parts)
valid_species <- sapply(strsplit(plant_for_phylo$species, " "), length) >= 2
plant_for_phylo <- plant_for_phylo[valid_species, ]

#--- 1d. phylogeny construction ------------------------------------------------

# Helper: wrapper around phylo.maker with error handling.
# scenario = 3 inserts species at the mid-point of the stem branch
# when the genus is present in the backbone tree.
construct_phylogeny <- function(species_data, tree, gen_list, scenario = 3) {
  tryCatch({
    phylo.maker(
      sp.list    = species_data,
      tree       = tree,
      gen.list   = gen_list,
      nodes.type = 1,
      scenario   = scenario
    )
  }, error = function(e) {
    cat("Phylogeny construction failed:", e$message, "\n")
    return(NULL)
  })
}

# Run phylogeny construction for the full species list
phylo_result <- construct_phylogeny(plant_for_phylo, megatree.Plants, gen.list.Plants)

# Save the Newick tree to disk
write.tree(phylo_result[[1]],
           "./Drift/Phylogeny/constructed_phylogeny.tre")


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: PLOT THE PHYLOGENY AT THE GENUS LEVEL 
# ==============================================================================

#--- 2a. Prune the phylogeny ---------------------------------------------------

# Extract genus from each tip label (format: "Genus_species")
genus <- sub("_.*", "", tree$tip.label)

# Retain one tip per genus (first occurrence)
keep_tips  <- tree$tip.label[!duplicated(genus)]
tree_genus <- keep.tip(tree, keep_tips)

# Rename tips to genus only for readability
tree_genus$tip.label <- sub("_.*", "", tree_genus$tip.label)

#--- 2b. Plot ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Fan plot: use.edge.length = FALSE gives an equal-branch cladogram,
# which is clearer when there are thousands of tips
plot(tree_genus,
     type           = "f",
     use.edge.length = FALSE,
     root.edge      = FALSE,
     cex            = 0.1,
     x.lim          = c(-1, 1),
     y.lim          = c(-1, 1),
     main           = "Genus-level plant phylogeny")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-plot-genus-phylogeny
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Fan phylogram of plant genera represented in iNaturalist range data (one species retained per genus).
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-width: 10
#--- 0. Load packages
library(ape)

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: PLOT THE PHYLOGENY AT THE GENUS LEVEL 
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Load the phylogeny ---------------------------------------------------
tree <- read.tree("./Drift/Phylogeny/constructed_phylogeny.tre")

#--- 1b. Prune the phylogeny ---------------------------------------------------
# Extract genus from each tip label (format: "Genus_species")
genus <- sub("_.*", "", tree$tip.label)

# Retain one tip per genus (first occurrence)
keep_tips  <- tree$tip.label[!duplicated(genus)]
tree_genus <- keep.tip(tree, keep_tips)

# Rename tips to genus only for readability
tree_genus$tip.label <- sub("_.*", "", tree_genus$tip.label)

#--- 1c. Plot ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Fan plot: use.edge.length = FALSE gives an equal-branch cladogram,
# which is clearer when there are thousands of tips
plot(tree_genus,
     type           = "f",
     use.edge.length = FALSE,
     root.edge      = FALSE,
     cex            = 0.1,
     x.lim          = c(-1, 1),
     y.lim          = c(-1, 1),
     main           = "Genus-level plant phylogeny")
```

To estimate phylogenetic diversity (PD) in each spatial cell before and after removing at-risk species, the analysis proceeds in three stages. First, we load the required inputs: species range maps in raster format, the seed-disperser phylogeny, and the IUCN Red List status table. Second, we define a core PD function that serves as the computational unit of the analysis. This function iterates cell by cell across the raster: for each cell, it receives a presence–absence vector—coded as 1 (present), 0 (absent), or NA (no data)—and uses it to prune the full phylogeny to the subset of species occurring in that cell. From this pruned subtree, the function returns two metrics: species richness, as the number of present species, and phylogenetic diversity, calculated as the total branch length of the subtree, capturing the evolutionary distinctiveness of the local assemblage beyond simple species counts. Finally, we apply this function across all cells to generate spatially explicit estimates of PD for the full, unmodified assemblage of seed-dispersing vertebrates (@fig-PD_Current).

```{r}
#| label: pd-setup
#| eval: false

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: ESTIMATE PD FOR CURRENT ASSEMBLAGES 
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Load the distribution and phylogeny data
##Load all batch rasters into memory and stack them
plant_range_raster <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/iNaturalist_geomodel_Plantae_ALL.tif")

## Load the phylogeny
phylo_resultUse <- read.tree("./Drift/Phylogeny/constructed_phylogeny.tre")

# Keep only species present in both the raster stack and the phylogeny.
# The raster uses spaces; the phylogeny uses underscores — we standardise here.
spp_in_phylo <- gsub("_", " ", phylo_resultUse$tip.label)
spp_use_rast <- names(plant_range_raster) %in% spp_in_phylo

plant_range_raster_pruned <- plant_range_raster[[spp_use_rast]]

#---1b. Estimate Phylogenetic Diversity
# Helper: Estimate Phylogenetic Diversity for a single grid cell
#
# @param x       Numeric vector of presence (1) / absence (0) / NA values,
# one element per species.
# @param spp.names Character vector of species names corresponding to `x`.
# @param phylo_tree An object of class `phylo`.
# @return Named numeric vector: `sppRic` (species richness) and
# `PD` (sum of branch lengths in the pruned tree).

PD.perGrid.fnc <- function(x, spp.names, phylo_tree) {
  require(ape)
  
  # Return NA if all values are missing (e.g., ocean/masked cell)
  if (is.na(sum(x, na.rm = TRUE))) {
    return(c(sppRic = NA, PD = NA))
  }
  
  # Return zero if no species are present
  if (sum(x, na.rm = TRUE) == 0) {
    return(c(sppRic = 0, PD = 0))
  }
  
  # Prune phylogeny to present species and compute metrics
  spp_present <- spp.names[x == 1]
  tree_pruned <- keep.tip(phylo_tree, gsub(" ", "_", spp_present))
  
  return(c(
    sppRic = length(spp_present),
    PD     = sum(tree_pruned$edge.length)
  ))
}

names.use <- names(plant_range_raster_pruned)

# Initialise an ncpus-1 core cluster
ncpus <- max(1, detectCores() - 1)
cl <- makeCluster(ncpus, type = "PSOCK")

clusterExport(cl, c("PD.perGrid.fnc", "names.use", "phylo_resultUse"))
clusterEvalQ(cl, library(ape))

# Apply PD function over all cells using terra::app()
# Each cell returns a 2-element vector → output raster has 2 layers
PD_raster <- app(
  plant_range_raster_pruned,
  fun = function(x) PD.perGrid.fnc(x, names.use, phylo_resultUse),
  cores = cl
)

stopCluster(cl)

names(PD_raster) <- c("Species_Richness", "Phylogenetic_Diversity")

writeRaster(
  PD_raster,
  "./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster.tif",
  overwrite = TRUE,
  gdal = c("COMPRESS=LZW", "PREDICTOR=2")
)

# Apply land mask: ocean/non-plant cells become NA
PD_current <- mask(PD_current, plant_rast_template)


# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: PLOT CURRENT PD 
# ==============================================================================
#--- 2a. Load & Project Vector World Boundaries --------------------------------

# Download medium-resolution country polygons from Natural Earth
# (scale = "medium" ≈ 1:50m; use "large" for finer detail at the cost of speed)
world_sf <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# Convert sf → SpatVector (terra's native vector class) for compatibility
# with geom_spatvector() in tidyterra
world_vect <- vect(world_sf)

# Reproject to Eckert IV (equal-area pseudocylindrical):
#   - Preserves area relationships across the globe
#   - Standard choice for global thematic/choropleth maps
#   - PROJ string: +proj=eck4
world_eck4 <- project(world_vect, "+proj=eck4")

#--- 2a. Reproject the PD raster -----------------------------------------------
# Also reproject the PD raster to Eckert IV so raster + vector align correctly.
# method = "bilinear" is appropriate for continuous data (PD values).
# NOTE: If this step is skipped, tidyterra will attempt on-the-fly reprojection,
# which is slower and less controllable.
PD_current_eck4 <- project(PD_current, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")
# Compute Log10-Transformed PD (for plotting only)
# PD values often span several orders of magnitude across biomes.
# A log10 transformation compresses the range so colour gradients
# highlight variation in species-poor regions, not just the highest values.
# We store this as a new object to keep the raw data intact.
PD_log <- log10(PD_current_eck4)

#--- 2c. Build the Map ---------------------------------------------------------
ggplot() +

## Base world polygons (filled grey, no border)
  # Rendered first so they sit below the raster layer.
  # Provides visual context for ocean and landmass extent.
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +

## PD raster layer
  # geom_spatraster() from {tidyterra} plots SpatRaster objects natively.
  # The raster must already be in the same CRS as the base map (Eckert IV).
  geom_spatraster(data = PD_log) +

## Country border overlay
  # Drawn on top of raster so coastlines/borders remain legible.
  # fill = NA means only the outline stroke is visible.
  geom_spatvector(
    data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA
    ) +

## Continuous colour scale (whitebox "muted" palette)
  # whitebox palettes are designed for cartographic use:
  #   - perceptually uniform (equal steps in lightness)
  #   - print- and colourblind-friendly
  # "muted" = low saturation; appropriate for scientific figures.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,          # Transparent for masked (ocean/no-data) cells
    name     = "Log_10 PD", 

    # Colour bar guide: wide and thin for bottom placement
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,   # Centred title
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Explicit units prevent scaling issues
      barheight      = unit(3,  "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +

## Theme & layout
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
    plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
  )
+

## Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Phylogenetic Diversity",
    subtitle = "Current conditions · Eckert IV equal-area projection · log_10(PD)"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-PD_Current
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

# Phylogenetic Diversity (PD) Map — Current Conditions
# Visualization of global vascular plant PD in Eckert IV projection

# 2. Load & Prepare Raster Data

# Load the second layer of the PD raster (layer 2 = current-period PD values)
# PD (Phylogenetic Diversity) measures the total branch length in a
# phylogenetic tree connecting all species in a given grid cell.
PD_current <- rast("./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster.tif", lyrs = 2)
# NOTE: Use `lyrs =` (plural) — `lyr =` is deprecated in recent terra versions


# Load the plant presence/naturalised raster to use as a land/ocean mask.
# Only the first layer is needed (used purely as a spatial template).
# This restricts PD values to terrestrial cells where plant occurrence
# data exists, avoiding spurious values over ocean pixels.
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]

# Apply land mask: ocean/non-plant cells become NA
PD_current <- mask(PD_current, plant_rast_template)


# 3. Load & Project Vector World Boundaries

# Download medium-resolution country polygons from Natural Earth
# (scale = "medium" ≈ 1:50m; use "large" for finer detail at the cost of speed)
world_sf <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# Convert sf → SpatVector (terra's native vector class) for compatibility
# with geom_spatvector() in tidyterra
world_vect <- vect(world_sf)

# Reproject to Eckert IV (equal-area pseudocylindrical):
#   - Preserves area relationships across the globe
#   - Standard choice for global thematic/choropleth maps
#   - PROJ string: +proj=eck4
world_eck4 <- project(world_vect, "+proj=eck4")

# Also reproject the PD raster to Eckert IV so raster + vector align correctly.
# method = "bilinear" is appropriate for continuous data (PD values).
# NOTE: If this step is skipped, tidyterra will attempt on-the-fly reprojection,
# which is slower and less controllable.
PD_current_eck4 <- project(PD_current, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")


# 4. Compute Log10-Transformed PD (for plotting only)

# PD values often span several orders of magnitude across biomes.
# A log10 transformation compresses the range so colour gradients
# highlight variation in species-poor regions, not just the highest values.
# We store this as a new object to keep the raw data intact.
PD_log <- log10(PD_current_eck4)


# 5. Build the Map

ggplot() +

# --- 5a. Base world polygons (filled grey, no border) -------------------------
  # Rendered first so they sit below the raster layer.
  # Provides visual context for ocean and landmass extent.
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +

# --- 5b. PD raster layer ------------------------------------------------------
  # geom_spatraster() from {tidyterra} plots SpatRaster objects natively.
  # The raster must already be in the same CRS as the base map (Eckert IV).
  geom_spatraster(data = PD_log) +

# --- 5c. Country border overlay -----------------------------------------------
  # Drawn on top of raster so coastlines/borders remain legible.
  # fill = NA means only the outline stroke is visible.
  geom_spatvector(
    data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA
    ) +

# --- 5d. Continuous colour scale (whitebox "muted" palette) -------------------
  # whitebox palettes are designed for cartographic use:
  #   - perceptually uniform (equal steps in lightness)
  #   - print- and colourblind-friendly
  # "muted" = low saturation; appropriate for scientific figures.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,          # Transparent for masked (ocean/no-data) cells
    name     = "Log₁₀ PD", # Unicode subscript for a polished legend title

    # Colour bar guide: wide and thin for bottom placement
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,   # Centred title
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Explicit units prevent scaling issues
      barheight      = unit(3,  "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +

# --- 5e. Theme & layout -------------------------------------------------------
theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
  plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
  plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
  plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
) +

# --- 5f. Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Phylogenetic Diversity",
    subtitle = "Current conditions · Eckert IV equal-area projection · log₁₀(PD)"
  )

```

We compare the PD for all species to the situation when all threatened species are removed (**IUCN-filtered scenario**) and when a random subset of species are removed (**Null model**).

1.  **IUCN-filtered scenario**: remove species with EOO \< 100,000 km² (@fig-PD_no_IUCN)

```{r}
#| label: PD_no_IUCN
#| eval: false
# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: ESTIMATE PD FOR REDUCED ASSEMBLAGES 
# ==============================================================================

#--- 1a. Load data and Filter Endenger species ---------------------------------

# Load IUCN status table 
IUCN_status_Tbl <- read.csv("./Drift/IUCN/IUCN_status_Plants.csv", row.names = NULL)

# Retain only species with EOO ≥ 100,000 km² (i.e., remove threatened spp.)
IUCN_status_Tbl_pruned <- IUCN_status_Tbl[IUCN_status_Tbl$area.km2 > 100000, ]

# Intersect with phylogeny to ensure all retained species have tree tips
spp_use_IUCN <- IUCN_status_Tbl_pruned$species %in%
                  gsub("_", " ", phylo_resultUse$tip.label)

plant_richness_no_IUCN <- plant_range_raster_pruned[[spp_use_IUCN]]
names.use.IUCN <- names(plant_richness_no_IUCN)

#--- 1b. Run PD estimation on the threat-filtered assemblages ------------------

library(parallel)
# Initialise an ncpus-1 core cluster
ncpus <- max(1, detectCores() - 1)
cl <- makeCluster(ncpus, type = "PSOCK")

# load functions and packages on each core
clusterExport(cl, c("PD.perGrid.fnc", "names.use.IUCN", "phylo_resultUse"))
clusterEvalQ(cl, library(ape))

PD_raster_IUCN <- app(
  plant_richness_no_IUCN,
  fun = function(x) PD.perGrid.fnc(x, names.use.IUCN, phylo_resultUse),
  cores = cl
)

stopCluster(cl)

names(PD_raster_IUCN) <- c("Species_Richness", "Phylogenetic_Diversity")

writeRaster(
  PD_raster_IUCN,
  "./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster_IUCN.tif",
  overwrite = TRUE,
  gdal = c("COMPRESS=LZW", "PREDICTOR=2")
)

# Projects the world map into Eckert IV projection, suitable for global thematic maps
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]
PD_raster_IUCN <- mask(PD_raster_IUCN, plant_rast_template)
# Load & Project Vector World Boundaries

#==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: PLOT CURRENT PD 
# ==============================================================================
#--- 2a. Load & Project Vector World Boundaries --------------------------------

# Download medium-resolution country polygons from Natural Earth
# (scale = "medium" ≈ 1:50m; use "large" for finer detail at the cost of speed)
world_sf <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# Convert sf → SpatVector (terra's native vector class) for compatibility
# with geom_spatvector() in tidyterra
world_vect <- vect(world_sf)

# Reproject to Eckert IV (equal-area pseudocylindrical):
#   - Preserves area relationships across the globe
#   - Standard choice for global thematic/choropleth maps
#   - PROJ string: +proj=eck4
world_eck4 <- project(world_vect, "+proj=eck4")

# Also reproject the PD raster to Eckert IV so raster + vector align correctly.
# method = "bilinear" is appropriate for continuous data (PD values).
# NOTE: If this step is skipped, tidyterra will attempt on-the-fly reprojection,
# which is slower and less controllable.
PD_IUCN_eck4 <- project(PD_raster_IUCN, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")


#--- 2b. Compute Log10-Transformed PD (for plotting only) ----------------------

# PD values often span several orders of magnitude across biomes.
# A log10 transformation compresses the range so colour gradients
# highlight variation in species-poor regions, not just the highest values.
# We store this as a new object to keep the raw data intact.
PD_log <- log10(PD_IUCN_eck4)


#--- 2c. Build the Map ---------------------------------------------------------

ggplot() +
  
# --- 1a. Base world polygons (filled grey, no border) -------------------------
  # Rendered first so they sit below the raster layer.
  # Provides visual context for ocean and landmass extent.
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +
  
# --- 1b. PD raster layer ------------------------------------------------------
  # geom_spatraster() from {tidyterra} plots SpatRaster objects natively.
  # The raster must already be in the same CRS as the base map (Eckert IV).
  geom_spatraster(data = PD_log) +
  
# --- 1c. Country border overlay -----------------------------------------------
  # Drawn on top of raster so coastlines/borders remain legible.
  # fill = NA means only the outline stroke is visible.
  geom_spatvector(
    data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA
  ) +
  
# --- 1d. Continuous colour scale (whitebox "muted" palette) -------------------
  # whitebox palettes are designed for cartographic use:
  #   - perceptually uniform (equal steps in lightness)
  #   - print- and colourblind-friendly
  # "muted" = low saturation; appropriate for scientific figures.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,          # Transparent for masked (ocean/no-data) cells
    name     = "Log_10 PD", 
    
    # Colour bar guide: wide and thin for bottom placement
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,   # Centred title
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Explicit units prevent scaling issues
      barheight      = unit(3,  "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +
  
# --- 1e. Theme & layout -------------------------------------------------------
theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
  plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
  plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
  plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
) +
  
# --- 1f. Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Phylogenetic Diversity",
    subtitle = "After the removal of IUCN-threatened species"
  )


```

```{r}
#| label: fig-PD_no_IUCN
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

#==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: PLOT CURRENT PD 
# ==============================================================================
#--- 2a. Load & Project Vector World Boundaries --------------------------------

# Download medium-resolution country polygons from Natural Earth
# (scale = "medium" ≈ 1:50m; use "large" for finer detail at the cost of speed)
world_sf <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# Convert sf → SpatVector (terra's native vector class) for compatibility
# with geom_spatvector() in tidyterra
world_vect <- vect(world_sf)

# Reproject to Eckert IV (equal-area pseudocylindrical):
#   - Preserves area relationships across the globe
#   - Standard choice for global thematic/choropleth maps
#   - PROJ string: +proj=eck4
world_eck4 <- project(world_vect, "+proj=eck4")

# Mask out ocean cells using the template (which only covers land)
PD_raster_IUCN <- rast("./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster_IUCN.tif",
                       lyr=2)
# Projects the world map into Eckert IV projection, suitable for global thematic maps
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]
PD_raster_IUCN <- mask(PD_raster_IUCN, plant_rast_template)
# Load & Project Vector World Boundaries

# Also reproject the PD raster to Eckert IV so raster + vector align correctly.
# method = "bilinear" is appropriate for continuous data (PD values).
# NOTE: If this step is skipped, tidyterra will attempt on-the-fly reprojection,
# which is slower and less controllable.
PD_IUCN_eck4 <- project(PD_raster_IUCN, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")


# Compute Log10-Transformed PD (for plotting only)

# PD values often span several orders of magnitude across biomes.
# A log10 transformation compresses the range so colour gradients
# highlight variation in species-poor regions, not just the highest values.
# We store this as a new object to keep the raw data intact.
PD_log <- log10(PD_IUCN_eck4)


# Build the Map

ggplot() +
  
# --- 1a. Base world polygons (filled grey, no border) -------------------------
  # Rendered first so they sit below the raster layer.
  # Provides visual context for ocean and landmass extent.
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +
  
# --- 1b. PD raster layer ------------------------------------------------------
  # geom_spatraster() from {tidyterra} plots SpatRaster objects natively.
  # The raster must already be in the same CRS as the base map (Eckert IV).
  geom_spatraster(data = PD_log) +
  
# --- 1c. Country border overlay -----------------------------------------------
  # Drawn on top of raster so coastlines/borders remain legible.
  # fill = NA means only the outline stroke is visible.
  geom_spatvector(
    data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA
  ) +
  
# --- 1d. Continuous colour scale (whitebox "muted" palette) -------------------
  # whitebox palettes are designed for cartographic use:
  #   - perceptually uniform (equal steps in lightness)
  #   - print- and colourblind-friendly
  # "muted" = low saturation; appropriate for scientific figures.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,          # Transparent for masked (ocean/no-data) cells
    name     = "Log_10 PD", 
    
    # Colour bar guide: wide and thin for bottom placement
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,   # Centred title
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Explicit units prevent scaling issues
      barheight      = unit(3,  "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +
  
# --- 1e. Theme & layout -------------------------------------------------------
theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
  plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
  plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
  plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
) +
  
# --- 1f. Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Phylogenetic Diversity",
    subtitle = "After the removal of IUCN-threatened species"
  )



```

2.  **Null model**: 100 random removals of the same number of species as in scenario 1 (@fig-PD_Rnd)

```{r}
#| label: PD_Rnd
#| eval: false

# ==============================================================================
# SECTION 1: ESTIMATE PD for NULL ASSEMBLAGES 
# ==============================================================================

n_rnd    <- 100          # number of randomisations
n_retain <- nlyr(plant_richness_no_IUCN)  # species to keep each iteration

PD_raster_rnd_list <- vector("list", n_rnd)

for (j in seq_len(n_rnd)) {
  t_start <- Sys.time()
  
  # Draw a random species subset of the same size as the IUCN-filtered set.
  # Species are drawn from the phylogeny-matched raster (plant_range_raster_pruned),
  # so no additional phylogeny check is needed.
  spp_use_rnd <- sample(names(plant_range_raster_pruned), n_retain)
  plant_richness_rnd <- plant_range_raster_pruned[[spp_use_rnd]]
  names.use.rnd <- names(plant_richness_rnd)
  
  # Estimate PD for the random assemblage
  cl <- makeCluster(8)
  clusterExport(cl, c("PD.perGrid.fnc", "names.use.rnd", "phylo_resultUse"))
  clusterEvalQ(cl, library(ape))
  
  PD_raster_rnd_j <- app(
    plant_richness_rnd,
    fun = function(x) PD.perGrid.fnc(x, names.use.rnd, phylo_resultUse),
    cores = cl
  )
  
  stopCluster(cl)
  
  names(PD_raster_rnd_j) <- c("Species_Richness", "Phylogenetic_Diversity")
  PD_raster_rnd_list[[j]] <- PD_raster_rnd_j
  
}

# Extract only the PD layer from each randomisation and stack them
PD_raster_rnd <- do.call("c", lapply(PD_raster_rnd_list, function(x) x[[2]]))

writeRaster(
  PD_raster_rnd,
  "./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster_rnd.tif",
  overwrite = TRUE,
  gdal = c("COMPRESS=LZW", "PREDICTOR=2")
)

# Projects the world map into Eckert IV projection, suitable for global thematic maps
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]
PD_raster_rnd <- mask(PD_raster_rnd, plant_rast_template)
# Load & Project Vector World Boundaries

#==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: PLOT CURRENT PD 
# ==============================================================================
#--- 2a. Load & Project Vector World Boundaries --------------------------------

# Download medium-resolution country polygons from Natural Earth
# (scale = "medium" ≈ 1:50m; use "large" for finer detail at the cost of speed)
world_sf <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# Convert sf → SpatVector (terra's native vector class) for compatibility
# with geom_spatvector() in tidyterra
world_vect <- vect(world_sf)

# Reproject to Eckert IV (equal-area pseudocylindrical):
#   - Preserves area relationships across the globe
#   - Standard choice for global thematic/choropleth maps
#   - PROJ string: +proj=eck4
world_eck4 <- project(world_vect, "+proj=eck4")

#---2b. Also reproject the PD raster to Eckert IV so raster + vector align correctly.
# method = "bilinear" is appropriate for continuous data (PD values).
# NOTE: If this step is skipped, tidyterra will attempt on-the-fly reprojection,
# which is slower and less controllable.
PD_Rnd_eck4 <- project(PD_raster_Rnd, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")


#---2c. Compute Log10-Transformed PD (for plotting only) -----------------------

# PD values often span several orders of magnitude across biomes.
# A log10 transformation compresses the range so colour gradients
# highlight variation in species-poor regions, not just the highest values.
# We store this as a new object to keep the raw data intact.
PD_log <- log10(PD_Rnd_eck4)


#---2d. Build the Map ----------------------------------------------------------

ggplot() +
  
# --- 1a. Base world polygons (filled grey, no border) -------------------------
  # Rendered first so they sit below the raster layer.
  # Provides visual context for ocean and landmass extent.
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +
  
# --- 1b. PD raster layer ------------------------------------------------------
  # geom_spatraster() from {tidyterra} plots SpatRaster objects natively.
  # The raster must already be in the same CRS as the base map (Eckert IV).
  geom_spatraster(data = PD_log) +
  
# --- 1c. Country border overlay -----------------------------------------------
  # Drawn on top of raster so coastlines/borders remain legible.
  # fill = NA means only the outline stroke is visible.
  geom_spatvector(
    data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA
  ) +
  
# --- 1d. Continuous colour scale (whitebox "muted" palette) -------------------
  # whitebox palettes are designed for cartographic use:
  #   - perceptually uniform (equal steps in lightness)
  #   - print- and colourblind-friendly
  # "muted" = low saturation; appropriate for scientific figures.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,          # Transparent for masked (ocean/no-data) cells
    name     = "Log_10 PD", 
    
    # Colour bar guide: wide and thin for bottom placement
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,   # Centred title
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Explicit units prevent scaling issues
      barheight      = unit(3,  "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +
  
# --- 1e. Theme & layout -------------------------------------------------------
theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
  plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
  plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
  plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
) +
  
# --- 1f. Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Phylogenetic Diversity",
    subtitle = "After the Random removal of species"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-PD_Rnd
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

#==============================================================================
# SECTION 2: PLOT CURRENT PD 
# ==============================================================================
#--- 2a. Load & Project Vector World Boundaries --------------------------------

# Download medium-resolution country polygons from Natural Earth
# (scale = "medium" ≈ 1:50m; use "large" for finer detail at the cost of speed)
world_sf <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# Convert sf → SpatVector (terra's native vector class) for compatibility
# with geom_spatvector() in tidyterra
world_vect <- vect(world_sf)

# Reproject to Eckert IV (equal-area pseudocylindrical):
#   - Preserves area relationships across the globe
#   - Standard choice for global thematic/choropleth maps
#   - PROJ string: +proj=eck4
world_eck4 <- project(world_vect, "+proj=eck4")

#---2b.  Mask out ocean cells using the template (which only covers land)
PD_raster_Rnd <- mean(rast("./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster_rnd.tif"))
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]
PD_raster_Rnd <- mask(PD_raster_Rnd, plant_rast_template)

#---2c. Also reproject the PD raster to Eckert IV so raster + vector align correctly.
# method = "bilinear" is appropriate for continuous data (PD values).
# NOTE: If this step is skipped, tidyterra will attempt on-the-fly reprojection,
# which is slower and less controllable.
PD_Rnd_eck4 <- project(PD_raster_Rnd, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")


#---2d Compute Log10-Transformed PD (for plotting only) ------------------------

# PD values often span several orders of magnitude across biomes.
# A log10 transformation compresses the range so colour gradients
# highlight variation in species-poor regions, not just the highest values.
# We store this as a new object to keep the raw data intact.
PD_log <- log10(PD_Rnd_eck4)


#--- 2e. Build the Map ---------------------------------------------------------

ggplot() +
  
# --- 1a. Base world polygons (filled grey, no border) -------------------------
  # Rendered first so they sit below the raster layer.
  # Provides visual context for ocean and landmass extent.
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +
  
# --- 1b. PD raster layer ------------------------------------------------------
  # geom_spatraster() from {tidyterra} plots SpatRaster objects natively.
  # The raster must already be in the same CRS as the base map (Eckert IV).
  geom_spatraster(data = PD_log) +
  
# --- 1c. Country border overlay -----------------------------------------------
  # Drawn on top of raster so coastlines/borders remain legible.
  # fill = NA means only the outline stroke is visible.
  geom_spatvector(
    data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA
  ) +
  
# --- 1d. Continuous colour scale (whitebox "muted" palette) -------------------
  # whitebox palettes are designed for cartographic use:
  #   - perceptually uniform (equal steps in lightness)
  #   - print- and colourblind-friendly
  # "muted" = low saturation; appropriate for scientific figures.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette  = "muted",
    na.value = NA,          # Transparent for masked (ocean/no-data) cells
    name     = "Log_10 PD", 
    
    # Colour bar guide: wide and thin for bottom placement
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,   # Centred title
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Explicit units prevent scaling issues
      barheight      = unit(3,  "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +
  
# --- 1e. Theme & layout -------------------------------------------------------
theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
  plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
  plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
  plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
) +
  
# --- 1f. Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Global Vascular Plant Phylogenetic Diversity",
    subtitle = "After the Random removal of species"
  )
```

The **IUCN-filtered scenario** shows the expected change in PD due to the lost of engendered species. The **Null-model** **scenario** provides the expected PD under random loss, against which phylogeny non-randomness of the threatened set can be assessed - this simulates what PD would look like if the same number of species were removed at random (i.e., non-phylogenetically structured loss).

Comparing observed PD under the IUCN threat scenario directly to the null mean is useful, but the raw difference depends on the absolute magnitude of PD in each cell — cells with high PD will always show larger absolute losses. The **Standardized Effect Size (SES)** places the observed loss on a common scale by expressing it in units of the null standard deviation:

$$\text{SES}_{PD} = \frac{PD_{\text{obs}} - \overline{PD}_{\text{rand}}}{\sigma_{PD_{\text{rand}}}}$$

where $PD_{\text{obs}}$ is the PD retained after removing threatened species (difference of the PD of the full community and the PD of the community with out engendered species), $\overline{PD}_{\text{rand}}$ is the per-cell mean PD change across the 100 random removals, and $\sigma_{PD_{\text{rand}}}$ is the corresponding standard deviation - (mean/sd of the differences between the PD of the full community and the PD of the communities with random removals).

To summarise the results we map the SES. A diverging colour palette centred on zero makes it straightforward to identify cells where threat-driven PD loss is significantly greater (negative SES, warm colours) or smaller (positive SES, cool colours) than expected by chance (@fig-ses-map).

```{r}
#| label: ses-load
#| eval: false

# Mask out ocean cells using the template (which only covers land)
PD_raster_Rnd <- rast("./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster_rnd.tif")
# Compute per-cell mean, SD, and number of non-NA randomisations
PD_raster_rnd_summ <- app(
  PD_raster_Rnd,
  function(x) {
    c(Mean = mean(x,   na.rm = TRUE),
      SD   = sd(x,     na.rm = TRUE),
      n    = sum(!is.na(x)))
  }
)

names(PD_raster_rnd_summ) <- c("Mean_PD_random", "SD_PD_random", "n_valid")

writeRaster(
  PD_raster_rnd_summ,
  "./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster_rnd_summ.tif",
  overwrite = TRUE,
  gdal = c("COMPRESS=LZW", "PREDICTOR=2")
)


# Load the PD raster for the IUCN threat-filtered assemblage (Part 5)
PD_raster_IUCN <- rast("./Drift/Phylogeny//PD_raster_IUCN.tif")

# Load the null-model summary raster (mean and SD across 100 randomisations)
PD_raster_rnd_summ <- rast("./Drift/Phylogeny/PD_raster_rnd_summ.tif")

# Confirm layer names are as expected
names(PD_raster_IUCN)      # "Species_Richness", "Phylogenetic_Diversity"
names(PD_raster_rnd_summ)  <- c("Mean_PD_random", "SD_PD_random", "n_valid")

# Extract the PD layer from the IUCN-filtered raster
PD_obs <- PD_raster_IUCN[["Phylogenetic_Diversity"]]

# Extract null mean and SD from the randomisation summary
PD_rnd_mean <- PD_raster_rnd_summ[["Mean_PD_random"]]
PD_rnd_sd   <- PD_raster_rnd_summ[["SD_PD_random"]]

# Compute SES cell-by-cell:
#   SES = (PD_obs - mean(PD_rand)) / sd(PD_rand)
# Cells where SD = 0 (all randomisations identical, e.g. very depauperate
# assemblages) receive NA to avoid division by zero.
SES_PD <- (PD_obs - PD_rnd_mean) / PD_rnd_sd

# Assign a meaningful layer name
names(SES_PD) <- "SES_PD_loss"

# Save
writeRaster(
  SES_PD,
  "./Drift/Phylogeny/SES_PD_loss.tif",
  overwrite = TRUE,
  gdal = c("COMPRESS=LZW", "PREDICTOR=2")
)

# Mask ocean cells using the plant presence raster as a land template.
# Only cells with native plant occurrence data are retained.
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]
SES_PD_masked <- mask(SES_PD, plant_rast_template)

# Reproject raster to Eckert IV (equal-area) BEFORE plotting.
# Doing this explicitly is faster and more reliable than on-the-fly reprojection
# inside ggplot. method = "bilinear" is correct for continuous SES values.
SES_PD_eck4 <- project(SES_PD_masked, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")


# Load & Project World Boundaries

world_eck4 <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") |>
  vect() |>                              # sf → SpatVector (terra)
  project("+proj=eck4")                  # Reproject to Eckert IV


# Define Colour Scale Parameters

# SES is centred on 0 by construction, so a DIVERGING palette is correct:
#   cool colours  → negative SES (more PD loss than chance)
#   neutral/white → SES ≈ 0 (loss as expected under null model)
#   warm colours  → positive SES (less PD loss than chance)
#
# Display limits: cap at ±5 SD — extreme outliers exist but capping
# prevents them from collapsing all variation into a narrow colour band.
# Cells outside ±5 are coloured as the palette extremes (oob = squish).
SES_LIMITS <- c(-5, 5)
SES_BREAKS <- c(-5, -2, 0, 2, 5)


# Build the Map

ggplot() +
  
# --- 1a. Grey land base (rendered below raster) -------------------------------
  # Provides context for land areas where SES data is NA (no plant records).
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +
  
# --- 1b. SES-PD raster layer --------------------------------------------------
  geom_spatraster(data = SES_PD_eck4) +
  
# --- 1c. Diverging colour scale centred on 0 ----------------------------------
  # whitebox "pi_y_g" (pink–yellow–green) or "bl_yl_rd" (blue–yellow–red)
  # are both diverging palettes. "muted" is sequential, so we switch to
  # a diverging option that correctly encodes the signed SES metric.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette   = "muted",          # Blue (negative) → yellow (0) → red (positive)
    direction = -1,                  # Flip so blue = loss (negative), red = gain
    na.value  = NA,                  # Ocean / no-data cells stay transparent
    limits    = SES_LIMITS,
    breaks    = SES_BREAKS,
    labels    = SES_BREAKS,
    oob       = scales::squish,      # Clamp out-of-bounds values to palette extremes
    name      = "SES-PD",
    
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Device-independent units
      barheight      = unit(3,   "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +
  
# --- 1d. Country border overlay -----------------------------------------------
  # White thin lines stay visible over both cool and warm palette regions.
  geom_spatvector(data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA) +
  
# --- 1e. Theme ----------------------------------------------------------------
theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
  plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
  plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
  plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
) +
  
# --- 1f. Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Standardised Effect Size of Phylogenetic Diversity Loss",
    subtitle = "SES-PD · Values below -2 and above 2 show  non-random phylogenetic erosion"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-ses-map
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Standardized Effect Size of PD loss due to threatened species removal. Negative values (warm colours) indicate phylogenetically clustered threat — local floras where the threatened species are more evolutionarily distinct than expected by chance.
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 8

#  Load & Prepare Raster Data

# SES-PD: Standardised Effect Size of Phylogenetic Diversity loss.
# Each cell value = (observed PD loss − mean null PD loss) / SD null PD loss.
# Values << 0 indicate non-random phylogenetic erosion (closely related
# species lost disproportionately); values >> 0 indicate PD is buffered.
SES_PD <- rast("./Drift/Phylogeny/SES_PD_loss.tif")

# Mask ocean cells using the plant presence raster as a land template.
# Only cells with native plant occurrence data are retained.
plant_rast_template <- rast("./Drift/Distributions/rast_plant_pres_nat.tif")[[1]]
SES_PD_masked <- mask(SES_PD, plant_rast_template)

# Reproject raster to Eckert IV (equal-area) BEFORE plotting.
# Doing this explicitly is faster and more reliable than on-the-fly reprojection
# inside ggplot. method = "bilinear" is correct for continuous SES values.
SES_PD_eck4 <- project(SES_PD_masked, "+proj=eck4", method = "bilinear")


# Load & Project World Boundaries

world_eck4 <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf") |>
  vect() |>                              # sf → SpatVector (terra)
  project("+proj=eck4")                  # Reproject to Eckert IV


# Define Colour Scale Parameters

# SES is centred on 0 by construction, so a DIVERGING palette is correct:
#   cool colours  → negative SES (more PD loss than chance)
#   neutral/white → SES ≈ 0 (loss as expected under null model)
#   warm colours  → positive SES (less PD loss than chance)
#
# Display limits: cap at ±5 SD — extreme outliers exist but capping
# prevents them from collapsing all variation into a narrow colour band.
# Cells outside ±5 are coloured as the palette extremes (oob = squish).
SES_LIMITS <- c(-5, 5)
SES_BREAKS <- c(-5, -2, 0, 2, 5)


# Build the Map

ggplot() +
  
# --- 1a. Grey land base (rendered below raster) -------------------------------
  # Provides context for land areas where SES data is NA (no plant records).
  geom_spatvector(data  = world_eck4) +
  
# --- 1b. SES-PD raster layer --------------------------------------------------
  geom_spatraster(data = SES_PD_eck4) +
  
# --- 1c. Diverging colour scale centred on 0 ----------------------------------
  # whitebox "pi_y_g" (pink–yellow–green) or "bl_yl_rd" (blue–yellow–red)
  # are both diverging palettes. "muted" is sequential, so we switch to
  # a diverging option that correctly encodes the signed SES metric.
  scale_fill_whitebox_c(
    palette   = "muted",          # Blue (negative) → yellow (0) → red (positive)
    direction = -1,                  # Flip so blue = loss (negative), red = gain
    na.value  = NA,                  # Ocean / no-data cells stay transparent
    limits    = SES_LIMITS,
    breaks    = SES_BREAKS,
    labels    = SES_BREAKS,
    oob       = scales::squish,      # Clamp out-of-bounds values to palette extremes
    name      = "SES-PD",
    
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust    = 0.5,
      barwidth       = unit(120, "mm"),  # Device-independent units
      barheight      = unit(3,   "mm"),
      ticks.colour   = "white",
      frame.colour   = "grey40"
    )
  ) +
  
# --- 1d. Country border overlay -----------------------------------------------
  # White thin lines stay visible over both cool and warm palette regions.
  geom_spatvector(data      = world_eck4,
    fill      = NA) +
  
# --- 1e. Theme ----------------------------------------------------------------
theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",  # Position the color legend at bottom for space efficiency
  plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",  hjust = 0.5),
  plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
  plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
)+
  
# --- 1f. Labels ---------------------------------------------------------------
  labs(
    title    = "Standardised Effect Size of Phylogenetic Diversity Loss",
    subtitle = "SES-PD · Values below -2 and above 2 show  non-random phylogenetic erosion"
    )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conclusion

In this supplementary material, we have demonstrated a concrete implementation of the process-based framework outlined in the main text, illustrating how the community assembly processes driving ecological novelty can be operationalised using globally available biodiversity, distributional, and environmental data. Working through each of the four core processes in turn, we have shown how dispersal limitation, abiotic filtering, biotic interactions, and ecological drift can be translated into spatially explicit, quantifiable, and mappable indices that reveal where and how novel ecosystems are most likely to emerge.

For **dispersal**, we estimated the mismatch between projected climate displacement velocities under RCP 6.0 and the dispersal capacities of multiple functional groups producing maps of dispersal lag that identify regions where species are at greatest risk of failing to track environmental change. For **abiotic filtering**, we generated maps of climate novelty. For **biotic interactions**, we combined distributional data from PHYLACINE 1.2.1 and BirdLife International with IUCN Red List endangerment classifications to map the current diversity of seed-dispersing vertebrates, the fraction of that diversity facing elevated extinction risk. For **ecological drift**, we used iNaturalist range data for vascular plants alongside phylogenetic information from global plant phylogenies to compute spatially explicit standardised effect sizes of phylogenetic diversity (SES-PD).

Together, these four analytical threads operationalise the mapping framework proposed in the main text using a shared set of reproducible, openly available tools. Although the worked examples here focus on seed-dispersing vertebrates and vascular plants as tractable and data-rich case studies, the underlying logic applies equally to other interaction types and taxonomic groups. We therefore offer this implementation not as a definitive analysis, but as a reproducible and extensible template — one that can be adapted, deepened, and scaled as richer data, finer-resolution phylogenies, and more refined interaction databases become available.
